Concierge Medicine for Busy Professionals: Is It Right for You?
Busy professionals need same-day access, telehealth, and a doctor who knows them. Here's why concierge and DPC medicine is designed for exactly that.

## Quick Answer
Concierge and Direct Primary Care practices are built around the needs busy professionals actually have: same-day appointments, direct physician access, telehealth that works, and a doctor who knows your history — without the scheduling friction of a conventional practice.

## Explanation

Be honest: when did you last have a primary care appointment that didn't require rearranging your calendar, sitting in a waiting room for 40 minutes past your scheduled time, and then getting 12 minutes with a physician who seemed to be reading your chart for the first time?

For most busy professionals, the answer is "not recently" — and often the follow-up is "so I just don't go."

That's a problem. Skipping primary care doesn't make health issues disappear; it makes them more expensive, more complex, and harder to treat when they finally demand attention. The patients who end up in the emergency room at 11 PM are often the ones who kept putting off a 20-minute conversation with their doctor that would have caught something six months earlier.

The traditional primary care model simply doesn't work well for high-functioning people with demanding schedules. It was designed for a world where a 9 AM appointment meant you'd be seen at 9 AM, where the physician had time to think about your case, and where there was an actual ongoing relationship between patient and doctor. That world largely no longer exists in conventional practice.

**What Busy Professionals Actually Need from Primary Care**

Talk to any executive, entrepreneur, or working professional who values their health, and the wish list tends to be consistent:

*Predictable scheduling.* Not three weeks out, not two-hour blocks blocked off for a 20-minute visit, and not a 9 AM appointment that doesn't start until 9:48 AM. Same-day or next-day access when something comes up, and the ability to book quickly when you actually have a window.

*Telehealth that isn't a workaround.* Standalone telehealth services — the kind offered through insurance apps or retail health platforms — involve seeing a different provider every time, no continuity of record, and limited ability to manage anything beyond the most straightforward issues. What professionals actually need is telehealth with their own physician: someone who knows them, has their history, and can have a real conversation.

*Direct messaging.* Being able to send your doctor a message about a lab result or a medication question and receive a thoughtful answer the same day — not a phone tree, not a nurse relay system, not a callback in three business days — is genuinely valuable. It turns minor questions into minor questions instead of unresolved anxieties.

*A physician who knows them.* When your doctor knows that you travel internationally for work, that you've had elevated blood pressure under stress, that you have a family history of early cardiac disease, and that you're the type of person who will ignore symptoms until forced to address them — that context informs every interaction. It's the difference between generic advice and care that actually fits your life.

*Longer visits when needed.* Annual physicals for high-performing adults should include time for a real conversation about risk factors, preventive strategies, lifestyle considerations, and health goals. A 20-minute appointment can't do that. A 1-to-2-hour appointment with a physician who's been thinking about your care can.

**Preventive and Executive Health**

One of the most significant benefits of concierge-style primary care for professionals is access to proactive, comprehensive preventive care. A physician who has time and who knows your history can work through advanced lipid panels, inflammatory markers, metabolic screening, blood pressure optimization, and lifestyle-based risk reduction in a way that a rushed conventional visit simply cannot accommodate.

For many professionals, the most valuable health investment is catching problems early — when they're manageable, reversible, or preventable — rather than treating them when they've compounded into something serious. That kind of proactive medicine requires a relationship, not a transaction.

At Forward Family Medicine, this proactive approach is deepened by integrated board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine with Dr. Lieberman — evidence-based guidance on nutrition, movement, sleep, and stress built directly into your care, not referred out. For professionals managing cardiovascular risk, metabolic health, or the physiological effects of chronic work stress, this is a meaningful clinical differentiator.

**Time Is the Real Luxury**

People talk about concierge medicine as a luxury, and in one sense that's accurate — it's better than the standard experience. But framed differently, what you're really buying is time: your physician's time (to think carefully about your care), and your own time (not spent waiting, rescheduling, navigating phone trees, or deferring care because the system makes it too difficult to access).

For professionals whose time genuinely has economic value, the calculus is often obvious once they do it.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

Forward Family Medicine is designed specifically around the needs of working adults and professionals in the Wayne and Main Line area. Our membership gives you same-day and next-day appointments, direct access to your physician by phone and message, telehealth visits built into the model, and annual physicals that have time to actually cover what matters.

We've built a practice that works for people who can't afford to have their healthcare not work. Visit [/membership](/membership) for current membership details, or explore our [concierge medicine](/concierge-medicine) page for more on our approach.

## Practical Tips

- Block your annual physical on your calendar at the start of each year, the same way you'd schedule a business commitment — preventive care deferred indefinitely is care not received.
- Ask whether your prospective DPC practice offers telehealth as a standard part of the membership, not as an add-on, and clarify what types of visits can be handled virtually versus in person.
- If you travel frequently, ask about the practice's approach to caring for patients who are out of town — a good DPC physician can often manage a significant amount remotely.
- Direct messaging your physician (a feature of most DPC memberships) is valuable for minor questions that don't warrant an appointment — use it rather than letting things accumulate.
- Pair your DPC membership with a comprehensive annual physical that includes the labs you actually want: lipid panels, inflammatory markers, metabolic panels, and any screenings relevant to your family history.
- If your employer or business offers health reimbursement arrangement (HRA) funds, ask whether a DPC membership or associated costs can be covered.
- Give your physician full context about your work demands, stress levels, travel schedule, and lifestyle — this information directly affects the quality of care you receive.

## FAQ

**Q: What can I actually handle via telehealth or direct message versus in person?**
A: Telehealth and messaging work well for medication questions, minor illness triage, lab result review, follow-up on known conditions, mental health check-ins, and many prescription refills. In-person visits are better for new or complex symptoms, any physical examination finding, procedures, and anything requiring direct assessment. Your DPC physician will help you decide which is appropriate for a given situation.

**Q: I travel a lot — can a DPC practice actually serve me?**
A: Better than most conventional practices, in fact. Telehealth and direct messaging allow your physician to manage many issues regardless of where you are. For acute care needs while traveling, your DPC physician can often help triage whether something warrants an urgent care visit locally or can be managed remotely — and they'll have context that a stranger at a walk-in clinic won't.

**Q: How do I fit in an annual physical if my schedule is unpredictable?**
A: DPC practices generally offer more flexible scheduling than conventional practices. Because the physician isn't packing a waiting room full of patients, finding a time that works for you — including early morning or other accommodated times — is often possible. Ask during your initial conversation about scheduling flexibility.

**Q: What advanced preventive screenings should I be asking about?**
A: This depends on your age, sex, family history, and lifestyle factors. A comprehensive annual physical in a DPC setting typically includes a review of cardiovascular risk (including advanced lipid panels if indicated), metabolic markers, blood pressure assessment, relevant cancer screenings, and a conversation about lifestyle factors including sleep, stress, and exercise. Your physician can walk you through what's appropriate for your specific profile.

Concierge Medicine on the Main Line: What Philadelphia Suburbs Need to Know
Looking for a concierge doctor in Wayne, Radnor, or Villanova? Here's what Main Line PA patients should know about DPC and concierge medicine options.

## Quick Answer
Concierge and Direct Primary Care practices are growing along the Main Line and Philadelphia suburbs, offering busy professionals and families an alternative to rushed, hard-to-access conventional primary care. Forward Family Medicine in Wayne, PA brings this model to the heart of the Main Line.

## Explanation

The Philadelphia Main Line — the string of communities running northwest from the city along the old Pennsylvania Railroad corridor through Bryn Mawr, Wayne, Berwyn, Paoli, and beyond — has always been home to people with high expectations. High-achieving professionals, dual-income families, executives managing demanding schedules, and retirees who've spent decades building something worth protecting. These are people who are accustomed to finding the best option available and who understand the value of investing in things that matter.

And yet, for all of that, the primary care experience for most Main Line residents is indistinguishable from the primary care experience anywhere else in the country: a three-week wait for a routine appointment, a 40-minute sit in the waiting room with a magazine from 2023, and then 12 minutes with a physician who is already typing their next note before you finish your sentence.

It doesn't have to be that way. And increasingly, Main Line patients are discovering that it isn't.

**The Concierge Model Arrives in the Suburbs**

Concierge medicine and Direct Primary Care have expanded well beyond their early reputation as urban, elite-only offerings. Today, DPC practices in particular — which offer the concierge experience at a flat monthly fee without insurance billing — are operating throughout the Philadelphia suburbs. They're serving professionals, families, retirees, self-employed individuals, and anyone else who has decided that their primary care relationship is worth investing in.

The Main Line is a natural fit for this model. The demographics here — high rates of employer-sponsored insurance alongside significant self-employment and small business ownership, a culture of valuing expertise and access, and a geography where people are accustomed to driving five or ten minutes to get exactly what they're looking for — align well with what DPC delivers.

**What Main Line Patients Should Look for in a Concierge Practice**

If you're evaluating concierge or DPC practices in the Wayne, Radnor, Villanova, or broader Main Line area, a few criteria matter more than others:

*Panel size.* The most important number in any concierge practice is how many patients the physician currently sees. A practice with 600 patients can offer meaningfully better access than conventional care. But panel size matters along the full spectrum. At Forward Family Medicine, we cap our panel at approximately 250 patients per physician — roughly half the DPC average — because we believe that number is what genuine availability and true continuity actually require. When we say same-day access, we mean it structurally, not aspirationally.

*Physician continuity.* One of the primary values of concierge care is that you have a consistent relationship with one physician — not a rotating cast of providers. Ask how the practice handles physician absence, and make sure you understand who you'll be seeing.

*Clinical depth* — specifically, whether the practice offers integrated Lifestyle Medicine. Forward Family Medicine is the only DPC practice on the Main Line with in-person, board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine (Dr. Lieberman, board-eligible in Family Medicine and Lifestyle Medicine). This means nutrition, physical activity, sleep, and stress management are treated as clinical interventions — not handouts — and included in every membership at no additional cost.

*What the fee actually covers.* This varies more than you'd expect. Some DPC practices include labs; others offer wholesale pricing. Some include telehealth; others charge separately. Get a clear picture of the full scope before you compare prices.

*Geographic access and convenience.* For Main Line residents, a practice in Wayne is accessible whether you're coming from Radnor, Berwyn, Malvern, or Villanova. SEPTA's Paoli/Thorndale Regional Rail line runs through Wayne, and the practice is reachable by car from throughout Delaware and Chester counties.

**Why DPC Serves Main Line Professionals Particularly Well**

Many Main Line professionals are self-employed, own small businesses, or work in industries where their health directly affects their productivity. For this population, the economics of DPC paired with a high-deductible health plan often make more sense than a premium PPO, and the access model — same-day appointments, direct physician messaging, telehealth — fits their actual lives better than a practice that requires booking three weeks out.

The Main Line also has a significant population of patients managing chronic conditions alongside busy schedules: hypertension, diabetes, thyroid disorders, cardiac risk factors. DPC's model of unlimited primary care visits for a flat fee is purpose-built for ongoing chronic disease management without the financial friction of copays discouraging necessary follow-up.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

Forward Family Medicine is located in Wayne, PA — in the center of the Main Line — and serves patients throughout Delaware County, Chester County, and the Philadelphia suburbs. We're a Direct Primary Care practice, which means we offer the concierge experience at a monthly membership price that's designed to be realistic for working professionals and families.

Our patients get same-day and next-day appointments, direct access to their physician, telehealth when that's more convenient, and longer visits that actually allow us to address what matters. We know our patients by name, by history, and by context. That's the whole point.

We also offer something no other DPC practice on the Main Line provides: integrated, in-person, board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine. Dr. Lieberman's dual board eligibility in Family Medicine and Lifestyle Medicine — combined with his background in culinary arts and nutrition science — means every member has access to a physician who can translate the latest evidence on diet, movement, and metabolic health into a plan that actually works in real life.

Learn more about our approach on our [concierge medicine](/concierge-medicine) and [direct primary care](/direct-primary-care) pages, or visit [/membership](/membership) for details on our current membership structure.

## Practical Tips

- When researching concierge or DPC practices on the Main Line, search specifically for physician-owned independent practices — these tend to maintain smaller panels and more genuine physician access than practices owned by hospital systems.
- Ask whether the physician lives or practices locally and has a long-term commitment to the area — continuity of the practice matters as much as continuity of care.
- If you work in Philadelphia and live on the Main Line, consider whether the practice can accommodate telehealth during the workday — a major convenience for city workers.
- For families, look for DPC practices that see patients of all ages — having the whole family with one physician simplifies care coordination considerably.
- SEPTA Regional Rail access to Wayne makes the practice reachable without a car if you need it.
- Ask prospective practices about their approach to care coordination with Philadelphia-area specialists at Penn, Jefferson, Main Line Health, and Lankenau — this network knowledge matters.
- Don't delay joining because you're "basically healthy" — the right time to establish a relationship with a primary care physician is before you need them urgently.

## FAQ

**Q: Is there a DPC or concierge practice that serves Wayne, Radnor, Villanova, and surrounding Main Line communities?**
A: Yes — Forward Family Medicine is located in Wayne, PA and serves patients throughout the Main Line and Philadelphia suburb area. Learn more at [/membership](/membership).

**Q: Do Main Line concierge practices accept insurance at all?**
A: It depends on the model. Traditional concierge practices usually still accept and bill insurance on top of a retainer fee. DPC practices like Forward Family Medicine do not bill insurance for primary care — the monthly membership covers primary care services. Patients still maintain their own insurance for specialist care, hospitalizations, and other services outside the practice's scope.

**Q: Can I join a DPC practice if my employer provides insurance?**
A: Absolutely. DPC membership is compatible with any insurance plan. Many DPC patients have employer-sponsored insurance and use it for specialists, imaging, and hospital care, while their DPC membership covers all primary care needs. Some employers are beginning to offer DPC memberships as a supplemental benefit — worth asking your HR department about.

**Q: How is a Main Line DPC practice different from going to Penn Medicine or Main Line Health for primary care?**
A: Large health system primary care practices operate under the same constraints as any conventional practice — large patient panels, time pressure, insurance billing requirements. DPC is structurally different: smaller panel, no insurance billing overhead, and a model designed around patient access and relationship. The care philosophy is different, not just the price structure.

Quick Answer

Concierge and Direct Primary Care practices are growing along the Main Line and Philadelphia suburbs, offering busy professionals and families an alternative to rushed, hard-to-access conventional primary care. Forward Family Medicine in Wayne, PA brings this model to the heart of the Main Line.

Explanation

The Philadelphia Main Line — the string of communities running northwest from the city along the old Pennsylvania Railroad corridor through Bryn Mawr, Wayne, Berwyn, Paoli, and beyond — has always been home to people with high expectations. High-achieving professionals, dual-income families, executives managing demanding schedules, and retirees who’ve spent decades building something worth protecting. These are people who are accustomed to finding the best option available and who understand the value of investing in things that matter.

And yet, for all of that, the primary care experience for most Main Line residents is indistinguishable from the primary care experience anywhere else in the country: a three-week wait for a routine appointment, a 40-minute sit in the waiting room with a magazine from 2023, and then 12 minutes with a physician who is already typing their next note before you finish your sentence.

It doesn’t have to be that way. And increasingly, Main Line patients are discovering that it isn’t.

The Concierge Model Arrives in the Suburbs

Concierge medicine and Direct Primary Care have expanded well beyond their early reputation as urban, elite-only offerings. Today, DPC practices in particular — which offer the concierge experience at a flat monthly fee without insurance billing — are operating throughout the Philadelphia suburbs. They’re serving professionals, families, retirees, self-employed individuals, and anyone else who has decided that their primary care relationship is worth investing in.

The Main Line is a natural fit for this model. The demographics here — high rates of employer-sponsored insurance alongside significant self-employment and small business ownership, a culture of valuing expertise and access, and a geography where people are accustomed to driving five or ten minutes to get exactly what they’re looking for — align well with what DPC delivers.

What Main Line Patients Should Look for in a Concierge Practice

If you’re evaluating concierge or DPC practices in the Wayne, Radnor, Villanova, or broader Main Line area, a few criteria matter more than others:

Panel size. The most important number in any concierge practice is how many patients the physician currently sees. A practice with 600 patients can offer meaningfully better access than conventional care. But panel size matters along the full spectrum. At Forward Family Medicine, we cap our panel at approximately 250 patients per physician — roughly half the DPC average — because we believe that number is what genuine availability and true continuity actually require. When we say same-day access, we mean it structurally, not aspirationally.

Physician continuity. One of the primary values of concierge care is that you have a consistent relationship with one physician — not a rotating cast of providers. Ask how the practice handles physician absence, and make sure you understand who you’ll be seeing.

Clinical depth — specifically, whether the practice offers integrated Lifestyle Medicine. Forward Family Medicine is the only DPC practice on the Main Line with in-person, board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine (Dr. Lieberman, board-eligible in Family Medicine and Lifestyle Medicine). This means nutrition, physical activity, sleep, and stress management are treated as clinical interventions — not handouts — and included in every membership at no additional cost.

What the fee actually covers. This varies more than you’d expect. Some DPC practices include labs; others offer wholesale pricing. Some include telehealth; others charge separately. Get a clear picture of the full scope before you compare prices.

Geographic access and convenience. For Main Line residents, a practice in Wayne is accessible whether you’re coming from Radnor, Berwyn, Malvern, or Villanova. SEPTA’s Paoli/Thorndale Regional Rail line runs through Wayne, and the practice is reachable by car from throughout Delaware and Chester counties.

Why DPC Serves Main Line Professionals Particularly Well

Many Main Line professionals are self-employed, own small businesses, or work in industries where their health directly affects their productivity. For this population, the economics of DPC paired with a high-deductible health plan often make more sense than a premium PPO, and the access model — same-day appointments, direct physician messaging, telehealth — fits their actual lives better than a practice that requires booking three weeks out.

The Main Line also has a significant population of patients managing chronic conditions alongside busy schedules: hypertension, diabetes, thyroid disorders, cardiac risk factors. DPC’s model of unlimited primary care visits for a flat fee is purpose-built for ongoing chronic disease management without the financial friction of copays discouraging necessary follow-up.

How Forward Family Medicine Helps

Forward Family Medicine is located in Wayne, PA — in the center of the Main Line — and serves patients throughout Delaware County, Chester County, and the Philadelphia suburbs. We’re a Direct Primary Care practice, which means we offer the concierge experience at a monthly membership price that’s designed to be realistic for working professionals and families.

Our patients get same-day and next-day appointments, direct access to their physician, telehealth when that’s more convenient, and longer visits that actually allow us to address what matters. We know our patients by name, by history, and by context. That’s the whole point.

We also offer something no other DPC practice on the Main Line provides: integrated, in-person, board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine. Dr. Lieberman’s dual board eligibility in Family Medicine and Lifestyle Medicine — combined with his background in culinary arts and nutrition science — means every member has access to a physician who can translate the latest evidence on diet, movement, and metabolic health into a plan that actually works in real life.

Learn more about our approach on our concierge medicine and direct primary care pages, or visit /membership for details on our current membership structure.

Practical Tips

  • When researching concierge or DPC practices on the Main Line, search specifically for physician-owned independent practices — these tend to maintain smaller panels and more genuine physician access than practices owned by hospital systems.
  • Ask whether the physician lives or practices locally and has a long-term commitment to the area — continuity of the practice matters as much as continuity of care.
  • If you work in Philadelphia and live on the Main Line, consider whether the practice can accommodate telehealth during the workday — a major convenience for city workers.
  • For families, look for DPC practices that see patients of all ages — having the whole family with one physician simplifies care coordination considerably.
  • SEPTA Regional Rail access to Wayne makes the practice reachable without a car if you need it.
  • Ask prospective practices about their approach to care coordination with Philadelphia-area specialists at Penn, Jefferson, Main Line Health, and Lankenau — this network knowledge matters.
  • Don’t delay joining because you’re “basically healthy” — the right time to establish a relationship with a primary care physician is before you need them urgently.

Concierge Medicine vs. Direct Primary Care: What's the Real Difference?
Concierge medicine and DPC both offer better access to your doctor, but the cost and billing structures are very different. Here's a clear comparison.
concierge medicine vs direct primary care

## Quick Answer
Both models offer smaller patient panels and better access to your physician, but traditional concierge medicine typically charges a higher retainer and still bills insurance, while Direct Primary Care charges a flat monthly fee with no insurance billing — delivering a similar experience at a significantly lower cost.

## Explanation

The terms "concierge medicine" and "direct primary care" get used interchangeably in conversation, and the confusion is understandable. Both involve paying directly for primary care. Both promise a closer relationship with your physician. Both offer same-day appointments and direct access. But the underlying structures are quite different, and those differences have real consequences for what you pay and how the practice operates.

**Traditional Concierge Medicine**

Traditional concierge medicine emerged in the late 1990s, pioneered by practices catering to high-net-worth patients who wanted more than standard insurance-based care could provide. The model works like this: patients pay an annual or monthly retainer fee — typically ranging from $150 to $500 per month, or $1,500 to $6,000 or more per year — in exchange for enhanced services and access. That retainer buys the VIP experience: same-day appointments, the physician's personal cell number, unhurried visits, house calls in some cases, and a doctor who genuinely knows you.

The key detail: most traditional concierge practices still accept and bill insurance. The retainer covers the "access and amenity" layer; insurance is still billed for the actual clinical services rendered at each visit. This means the practice maintains all the billing infrastructure, coding staff, and administrative burden of a conventional practice — it's just layered on top of the retainer. For the physician, this is financially stable but operationally complex. For patients, it means the retainer is genuinely an additional cost on top of existing insurance premiums and copays.

**Direct Primary Care**

Direct Primary Care (DPC) took shape in the mid-2000s and represents a more radical departure from the insurance-based system. In a DPC practice, patients pay a flat monthly membership fee — typically $75 to $200 per month for adults depending on the practice, location, and what is included. At Forward Family Medicine, individual membership is $200/month — which includes integrated board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine that most DPC practices do not offer — and the practice does not bill insurance for any primary care services. The monthly fee covers office visits, most in-office procedures, care coordination, direct physician access, and often basic lab work. Period.

Because DPC practices opt out of insurance billing entirely, they eliminate the administrative overhead that consumes 25 to 30 percent of revenue in conventional practices. No billing staff. No coding audits. No pre-authorization calls. No claims denials to fight. That overhead reduction is what makes the DPC membership price so much lower than the traditional concierge retainer — not a lesser level of care, but a leaner back office.

**The experience for patients is remarkably similar.** In both models, you get:

- A physician with a small patient panel (typically 300–600 patients in DPC generally, though at Forward Family Medicine we cap our panel at 250 patients per physician — smaller than the DPC average, which is what makes same-day access consistently reliable — versus 1,500–2,500 in conventional practices)
- Same-day or next-day appointment availability
- Extended visits with time to actually talk
- Direct physician access by phone, text, or email
- A care relationship built on continuity

**Where they diverge:**

The biggest practical difference is cost and complexity. Traditional concierge can run $3,000 to $6,000 per year just for the retainer, before insurance premiums, copays, and deductibles. DPC typically costs $900 to $1,800 per year in membership fees, and because primary care visits are covered, patients can often pair a DPC membership with a high-deductible health plan — keeping total healthcare spending lower than a traditional PPO arrangement.

There's also a philosophical difference. DPC practices are explicitly outside the insurance system for primary care; the physician's financial incentive is your continued satisfaction and health, not maximizing billable encounters. Traditional concierge practices, because they still bill insurance, retain some of the fee-for-service incentive structure.

**Which is better for you?**

At Forward Family Medicine, we don't think of DPC and concierge as a tradeoff. Every member receives what the industry would call a concierge experience — a panel capped at 250 patients, a doctor who picks up the phone, 1–2 hour visits, and direct access by phone, text, and email. We've simply structured it without the insurance billing overhead that inflates the traditional concierge retainer. The result is a concierge-quality experience at a DPC price point.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

Forward Family Medicine is a Direct Primary Care practice, which means we've structured everything around the flat-monthly-membership model. No insurance billing for primary care, no per-visit charges on top of your fee, and no surprise bills for routine care. Our membership covers the primary care services you actually need.

We describe our approach in detail on both our [concierge medicine](/concierge-medicine) and [direct primary care](/direct-primary-care) pages. If you've been researching concierge practices in the Wayne and Main Line area and wondering whether DPC might serve you just as well at a more accessible price point, we'd be glad to walk you through what our membership covers. Visit [/membership](/membership) for details.

## Practical Tips

- When evaluating any concierge or DPC practice, ask explicitly: "Do you still bill insurance for visits, or is everything covered by the membership?" — the answer tells you which model you're looking at.
- Calculate your true annual cost: traditional concierge retainer plus insurance premiums plus expected copays/deductibles, versus DPC membership plus an HDHP premium.
- For patients with one or more chronic conditions requiring frequent primary care visits, DPC often delivers the greatest financial benefit — all those visits are covered in the flat fee.
- DPC pairs well with a health-sharing ministry membership or a low-premium catastrophic plan for patients who are otherwise healthy.
- Ask about panel size at any practice you're considering — this is the single most important determinant of whether you'll actually get same-day access.
- Some employers are beginning to offer DPC memberships as a benefit; ask your HR department if this is an option.
- Don't overlook direct messaging and telehealth as part of the value equation — for many patients, asynchronous access to their physician is worth as much as in-person visits.

## FAQ

**Q: Can I use insurance at a DPC practice for anything?**
A: DPC practices don't bill insurance for primary care services, but patients still use their insurance for outside services: specialist visits, hospitalizations, imaging, procedures, prescriptions, and emergency care. Most DPC patients maintain health insurance for those needs.

**Q: Is DPC only for healthy people who rarely need care?**
A: Actually the opposite is often true. Patients with chronic conditions like diabetes, hypertension, or thyroid disorders frequently benefit the most from DPC because they need regular primary care visits — which are all included in the flat fee. The continuity and access also supports better chronic disease management.

**Q: Why do some DPC practices call themselves "concierge" and vice versa?**
A: The terminology has never been precisely regulated, and marketing language varies by practice. The most reliable way to understand any practice is to ask directly: what does the membership fee include, do you bill insurance, and what is the current panel size?

**Q: If I join a DPC practice, do I still need to pay for lab work and imaging?**
A: It depends on the practice. Many DPC practices include basic labs in the membership or negotiate deeply discounted rates for members. Imaging and specialist visits are generally separate and would be billed through your insurance or paid at negotiated cash-pay rates. Ask any prospective DPC practice to walk you through their specific inclusions and the costs for common services outside the membership.

The Diabetes Prevention Program: What It Is and Who Should Do It
The CDC-recognized Diabetes Prevention Program reduces type 2 diabetes risk by 58%. Learn who qualifies, what the program involves, and how DPC supports it.

## Quick Answer
The National Diabetes Prevention Program (NDPP) is a CDC-recognized, evidence-based lifestyle change program proven to reduce the risk of developing type 2 diabetes by 58% in people with prediabetes — and by 71% in adults over 60. If you have prediabetes or are at elevated risk, this program is one of the most powerful interventions available.

## Explanation

Type 2 diabetes is not inevitable. For the roughly 98 million American adults with prediabetes — about 1 in 3 — the trajectory toward diabetes is not fixed. It can be interrupted. The National Diabetes Prevention Program (NDPP) is the most rigorously studied lifestyle intervention we have for doing exactly that.

**The evidence behind the program:**

The NDPP grew out of the landmark Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP) clinical trial, funded by the NIH and published in the New England Journal of Medicine in 2002. The trial enrolled over 3,000 adults with prediabetes and randomly assigned them to three groups: intensive lifestyle intervention, metformin (a diabetes medication), or placebo.

Results after an average of 2.8 years were striking:
- The lifestyle intervention group reduced their risk of developing type 2 diabetes by **58%** compared to placebo
- The metformin group reduced risk by **31%** compared to placebo
- In adults aged 60 and older, the lifestyle intervention reduced risk by **71%**

The lifestyle program was more effective than medication — and the benefits persisted in long-term follow-up studies. This is not a marginal improvement; it is a profound one.

**What the NDPP program actually involves:**

The CDC-recognized NDPP is a structured 12-month lifestyle change program delivered by trained lifestyle coaches. It consists of:
- **16 weekly core sessions** (approximately the first 6 months) focused on healthy eating, physical activity, stress management, and problem-solving
- **6 or more monthly follow-up sessions** (the second 6 months) to reinforce and maintain changes

The two primary goals of the program:
1. Achieve and maintain at least 5–7% body weight loss
2. Accumulate at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity physical activity

Programs are available in-person, online, and through a combination of both — making it accessible even for people with demanding schedules. Many employers and health insurers now cover CDC-recognized NDPP programs. Medicare covers the program for eligible beneficiaries (those with a positive prediabetes screening and a BMI of 25 or higher, or 23 or higher for Asian Americans).

**Who qualifies:**

You are eligible for the NDPP if you have:
- Prediabetes, defined as an HbA1c of 5.7–6.4%, a fasting plasma glucose of 100–125 mg/dL, or a 2-hour plasma glucose of 140–199 mg/dL on an oral glucose tolerance test
- A BMI of 25 or higher (23 or higher if you are of Asian descent)
- No previous diagnosis of type 2 diabetes
- Not been pregnant in the past year (gestational diabetes history actually qualifies you — it is a significant risk factor)

Even if you do not strictly meet the above criteria, you may benefit from a similar lifestyle intervention if you have multiple metabolic risk factors. Your physician can help assess your individual situation.

**Why the 5–7% weight loss goal matters:**

The NDPP's weight loss goal — modest by most standards — is specifically calibrated to reduce insulin resistance and improve beta cell function in the pancreas. You do not need to lose 50 pounds. A 150-pound person losing 8–10 pounds, combined with consistent physical activity, produces meaningful metabolic change. The program is not about aesthetics; it is about restoring insulin sensitivity.

**What happens if prediabetes is not addressed:**

Without intervention, approximately 15–30% of people with prediabetes will develop type 2 diabetes within 5 years. Type 2 diabetes substantially increases the risk of heart disease, stroke, kidney failure, nerve damage, and blindness — all largely preventable with early intervention. Prediabetes itself is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, independent of whether diabetes develops.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

Identifying prediabetes is step one. Knowing what to do about it — and having the support to actually do it — is where most patients get stuck. At Forward Family Medicine, we screen for prediabetes routinely, interpret your results in clinical context, and have a real conversation about your options when results are elevated.

We can refer you to CDC-recognized NDPP programs in the area and through online platforms, and we follow your progress over time with repeat lab testing and lifestyle check-ins. Through our [direct primary care](/direct-primary-care) model, you have ongoing access to your physician by phone or message — so if you hit a plateau or have questions mid-program, you do not have to wait for a scheduled appointment.

We also integrate diabetes prevention into our broader [lifestyle medicine](/lifestyle-medicine) approach. Prediabetes does not exist in isolation — it is typically accompanied by other metabolic risk factors like hypertension, high triglycerides, and low HDL. Addressing them together produces compounding benefits.

For patients who prefer a more structured individual approach or who have already progressed to type 2 diabetes, we offer comprehensive metabolic management as part of your [membership](/membership). Prevention is the goal, but we meet you wherever you are.

## Practical Tips

- Ask your physician to check your HbA1c or fasting glucose if you have not had one recently — prediabetes has no symptoms and is frequently undiagnosed
- If you qualify for NDPP, look for a CDC-recognized program through the CDC's online directory at prevent.diabetes.org
- The 150 minutes of weekly activity goal breaks down to about 30 minutes, 5 days per week — brisk walking counts
- Focusing on the first 5–7% of body weight loss has the biggest metabolic payoff; do not let perfect be the enemy of good
- Sleep deprivation and chronic stress both increase insulin resistance — the NDPP lifestyle sessions address these, not just diet and exercise
- If your employer offers wellness programs, ask whether they cover an NDPP-recognized program
- Gestational diabetes is a strong predictor of future type 2 diabetes — if you had it, prioritize screening and risk reduction even if your postpartum glucose was normal

## FAQ

**Q: I was told I have "borderline" diabetes. Is that the same as prediabetes?**
A: Yes — "borderline diabetes," "impaired fasting glucose," and "impaired glucose tolerance" are all terms that describe prediabetes. Prediabetes is defined by specific lab thresholds: HbA1c 5.7–6.4%, fasting glucose 100–125 mg/dL, or a 2-hour glucose of 140–199 mg/dL. If any of these apply to you, you likely qualify for the NDPP and would benefit significantly from lifestyle intervention.

**Q: Is the Diabetes Prevention Program the same as a diet program?**
A: It incorporates dietary guidance, but it is much more comprehensive than a diet program. The NDPP addresses physical activity, stress management, sleep, problem-solving, and behavior change strategies. The lifestyle coach model — trained, evidence-based, structured — is what distinguishes it from general wellness advice. The curriculum was developed through NIH-funded research and is consistently delivered by certified coaches.

**Q: My A1c is 5.6%. Should I be concerned?**
A: An HbA1c of 5.6% is at the upper edge of normal (prediabetes begins at 5.7%). While 5.6% is not technically prediabetes, it is worth monitoring closely — especially if you have other risk factors like obesity, family history of diabetes, or elevated triglycerides. Annual repeat testing and proactive lifestyle attention are appropriate. A conversation with your physician can put your specific result in context.

**Q: Can metformin replace the lifestyle program?**
A: Metformin is an option, but the DPP trial showed it was roughly half as effective as the lifestyle intervention (31% vs. 58% risk reduction). The lifestyle program also produces additional benefits — cardiovascular risk reduction, improved energy, better sleep — that medication does not replicate. Some physicians recommend metformin in addition to lifestyle intervention for higher-risk patients, but it is not a substitute for the program.

The CDC-recognized NDPP is a structured 12-month lifestyle change program delivered by trained lifestyle coaches. It consists of:

  • 16 weekly core sessions (approximately the first 6 months) focused on healthy eating, physical activity, stress management, and problem-solving
  • 6 or more monthly follow-up sessions (the second 6 months) to reinforce and maintain changes

The two primary goals of the program:

  1. Achieve and maintain at least 5–7% body weight loss
  2. Accumulate at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity physical activity

Programs are available in-person, online, and through a combination of both — making it accessible even for people with demanding schedules. Many employers and health insurers now cover CDC-recognized NDPP programs. Medicare covers the program for eligible beneficiaries (those with a positive prediabetes screening and a BMI of 25 or higher, or 23 or higher for Asian Americans).

Who qualifies:

You are eligible for the NDPP if you have:

  • Prediabetes, defined as an HbA1c of 5.7–6.4%, a fasting plasma glucose of 100–125 mg/dL, or a 2-hour plasma glucose of 140–199 mg/dL on an oral glucose tolerance test
  • A BMI of 25 or higher (23 or higher if you are of Asian descent)
  • No previous diagnosis of type 2 diabetes
  • Not been pregnant in the past year (gestational diabetes history actually qualifies you — it is a significant risk factor)

Even if you do not strictly meet the above criteria, you may benefit from a similar lifestyle intervention if you have multiple metabolic risk factors. Your physician can help assess your individual situation.

Why the 5–7% weight loss goal matters:

The NDPP’s weight loss goal — modest by most standards — is specifically calibrated to reduce insulin resistance and improve beta cell function in the pancreas. You do not need to lose 50 pounds. A 150-pound person losing 8–10 pounds, combined with consistent physical activity, produces meaningful metabolic change. The program is not about aesthetics; it is about restoring insulin sensitivity.

What happens if prediabetes is not addressed:

Without intervention, approximately 15–30% of people with prediabetes will develop type 2 diabetes within 5 years. Type 2 diabetes substantially increases the risk of heart disease, stroke, kidney failure, nerve damage, and blindness — all largely preventable with early intervention. Prediabetes itself is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, independent of whether diabetes develops.

How Forward Family Medicine Helps

Identifying prediabetes is step one. Knowing what to do about it — and having the support to actually do it — is where most patients get stuck. At Forward Family Medicine, we screen for prediabetes routinely, interpret your results in clinical context, and have a real conversation about your options when results are elevated.

We can refer you to CDC-recognized NDPP programs in the area and through online platforms, and we follow your progress over time with repeat lab testing and lifestyle check-ins. Through our direct primary care model, you have ongoing access to your physician by phone or message — so if you hit a plateau or have questions mid-program, you do not have to wait for a scheduled appointment.

We also integrate diabetes prevention into our broader lifestyle medicine approach. Prediabetes does not exist in isolation — it is typically accompanied by other metabolic risk factors like hypertension, high triglycerides, and low HDL. Addressing them together produces compounding benefits.

For patients who prefer a more structured individual approach or who have already progressed to type 2 diabetes, we offer comprehensive metabolic management as part of your membership. Prevention is the goal, but we meet you wherever you are.

Practical Tips

  • Ask your physician to check your HbA1c or fasting glucose if you have not had one recently — prediabetes has no symptoms and is frequently undiagnosed
  • If you qualify for NDPP, look for a CDC-recognized program through the CDC’s online directory at prevent.diabetes.org
  • The 150 minutes of weekly activity goal breaks down to about 30 minutes, 5 days per week — brisk walking counts
  • Focusing on the first 5–7% of body weight loss has the biggest metabolic payoff; do not let perfect be the enemy of good
  • Sleep deprivation and chronic stress both increase insulin resistance — the NDPP lifestyle sessions address these, not just diet and exercise
  • If your employer offers wellness programs, ask whether they cover an NDPP-recognized program
  • Gestational diabetes is a strong predictor of future type 2 diabetes — if you had it, prioritize screening and risk reduction even if your postpartum glucose was normal

FAQ

Q: I was told I have “borderline” diabetes. Is that the same as prediabetes? A: Yes — “borderline diabetes,” “impaired fasting glucose,” and “impaired glucose tolerance” are all terms that describe prediabetes. Prediabetes is defined by specific lab thresholds: HbA1c 5.7–6.4%, fasting glucose 100–125 mg/dL, or a 2-hour glucose of 140–199 mg/dL. If any of these apply to you, you likely qualify for the NDPP and would benefit significantly from lifestyle intervention.

Q: Is the Diabetes Prevention Program the same as a diet program? A: It incorporates dietary guidance, but it is much more comprehensive than a diet program. The NDPP addresses physical activity, stress management, sleep, problem-solving, and behavior change strategies. The lifestyle coach model — trained, evidence-based, structured — is what distinguishes it from general wellness advice. The curriculum was developed through NIH-funded research and is consistently delivered by certified coaches.

Q: My A1c is 5.6%. Should I be concerned? A: An HbA1c of 5.6% is at the upper edge of normal (prediabetes begins at 5.7%). While 5.6% is not technically prediabetes, it is worth monitoring closely — especially if you have other risk factors like obesity, family history of diabetes, or elevated triglycerides. Annual repeat testing and proactive lifestyle attention are appropriate. A conversation with your physician can put your specific result in context.

Q: Can metformin replace the lifestyle program? A: Metformin is an option, but the DPP trial showed it was roughly half as effective as the lifestyle intervention (31% vs. 58% risk reduction). The lifestyle program also produces additional benefits — cardiovascular risk reduction, improved energy, better sleep — that medication does not replicate. Some physicians recommend metformin in addition to lifestyle intervention for higher-risk patients, but it is not a substitute for the program.
Can You Use an HSA with Direct Primary Care? What You Need to Know
DPC membership fees are not currently HSA-eligible, but labs, medications, and procedures often are. Here's the full picture on HSA and DPC — and the strategy that works.
can you use HSA for DPC

## Quick Answer
As of January 1, 2026, the Primary Care Enhancement Act clarified that HSA funds may be used for DPC membership fees, removing the previous uncertainty under IRS Notice 2004-23. If you are pairing a DPC membership with a qualifying high-deductible health plan (HDHP), you can now use your HSA to cover your DPC membership fees. Confirm specifics with your plan administrator.

## Explanation

Health savings accounts are one of the best financial tools available for managing healthcare costs. Triple tax benefit: contributions go in pre-tax, money grows tax-free, and withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free. If you're pairing a DPC membership with a high-deductible health plan (HDHP) — the smart strategy for many people — you're likely eligible to contribute to an HSA.

But the DPC-HSA relationship has a catch that's worth understanding clearly.

### The HSA Eligibility Question for DPC Membership Fees

The IRS determines what counts as a "qualified medical expense" eligible for HSA reimbursement. In general, insurance premiums are not eligible (with some exceptions), while direct medical expenses are. DPC membership fees fall into a gray zone — they're not insurance premiums, but they're also not a fee-for-service medical expense.

The IRS issued guidance (Notice 2004-23) stating that amounts paid to a DPC-type provider that covers multiple services are generally not HSA-qualified expenses. The reasoning: because DPC covers a broad range of primary care services for a flat fee, it functions more like a prepaid health plan than a direct medical expense — and prepaid health plan fees are not HSA-eligible.

**The practical takeaway:** Do not assume your DPC membership fee can be paid with HSA funds without consulting a tax advisor. The mainstream interpretation is that it cannot, and using HSA funds for ineligible expenses creates a tax liability plus penalty.

**Update as of January 1, 2026:** The Primary Care Enhancement Act, which took effect January 1, 2026, explicitly addressed this gray zone by clarifying that DPC membership fees are qualified medical expenses eligible for HSA reimbursement. This is a significant change that resolves the prior ambiguity. If your HDHP and HSA were established before this date, confirm with your plan administrator how this applies to your specific account. Going forward, DPC members on qualifying HDHPs can use HSA funds for their monthly membership fees.

### What IS HSA-Eligible at a DPC Practice

While the membership fee itself is the sticking point, many things you access through a DPC practice are clearly HSA-eligible as direct medical expenses:

- **Lab fees** — if your DPC practice charges separately for labs (rather than including them in membership), those costs are qualified medical expenses
- **Prescription medications** — drugs dispensed by your DPC practice or obtained with a prescription are HSA-eligible
- **Procedures** — any procedure billed separately (wound care, injections, minor surgery) is typically HSA-eligible
- **Imaging** — X-rays or other imaging ordered through your DPC practice and billed separately are eligible
- **Specialist visits** — any specialist visits covered by your health insurance remain HSA-eligible as standard cost-sharing

This is meaningful. If your DPC practice offers wholesale lab pricing and in-office medication dispensing — as many do — you can pay for those services with tax-advantaged HSA dollars, capturing the wholesale pricing savings plus the tax benefit.

### The DPC + HDHP + HSA Strategy

Despite the membership fee limitation, the combined DPC + HDHP + HSA strategy remains excellent for most patients:

**Step 1: Enroll in DPC** for unlimited primary care at a predictable monthly cost — paid from your regular (post-tax) income.

**Step 2: Carry a qualifying HDHP** for catastrophic coverage. HDHPs are the only insurance plans that allow HSA contributions under current law. They also carry the lowest monthly premiums, freeing up cash flow.

**Step 3: Maximize your HSA contribution** each year. In 2026, the contribution limits are $4,300 for individuals and $8,550 for families. This money can cover your deductible if a major health event occurs, pay for HSA-eligible services at your DPC practice, cover dental, vision, mental health, and other qualified expenses, and grow tax-free as an investment account if you choose not to spend it immediately.

**Step 4: Use your DPC membership for primary care** — avoiding the smaller primary care claims that would drain your HSA and your deductible unnecessarily.

The result: your primary care is excellent and accessible (DPC), your catastrophic exposure is covered (HDHP), and you're building a growing tax-advantaged reserve for medical expenses (HSA). It's genuinely a well-designed healthcare financial strategy.

### What Would Change: The AHLCA Act

The American Health Care Affordability Act (and similar legislation) has been introduced in Congress multiple times to explicitly make DPC membership fees HSA-eligible. The legislation has bipartisan support in concept — the argument is straightforward: DPC patients are taking significant primary care costs off the insurance system, and allowing HSA contributions to cover DPC fees would encourage this behavior and expand access to DPC.

Update: The Primary Care Enhancement Act became effective January 1, 2026, resolving the HSA eligibility question for most DPC members. The AHLCA and similar earlier proposals are now moot — the key legislation passed. If you enrolled in DPC before 2026 and want to know whether you can retroactively reimburse 2025 membership fees, consult a tax advisor — the Act is prospective.

### Practical Guidance

- **Do not use HSA funds for your monthly DPC membership fee** without explicit written guidance from a qualified tax advisor confirming eligibility in your specific situation.
- **Do use HSA funds for labs, medications, and procedures** obtained through your DPC practice — these are generally qualified expenses.
- **Keep receipts and documentation** for any DPC-adjacent expenses you pay with HSA funds, in case of audit.
- **Contribute the IRS maximum to your HSA** each year if you're on an HDHP — the triple tax benefit compounds over time and creates meaningful financial protection.
- **Consult a CPA or tax advisor** who understands healthcare expenses before making HSA decisions — the rules are nuanced and the penalties for errors are real.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

We understand that healthcare finances are complex, and we help our members navigate the practical side of DPC, insurance, and HSA strategy. When you join Forward Family Medicine, we're transparent about what services may be billed separately (and thus HSA-eligible) versus what's covered in your membership.

Our wholesale lab pricing and in-office medication dispensing create additional opportunities to use HSA funds effectively — accessing high-quality services at dramatically reduced prices and paying with pre-tax dollars.

For a full picture of what's included in membership and what might be billed separately, visit our [membership page](/membership). For more about how the DPC model works financially, visit our [direct primary care overview](/direct-primary-care).

## Practical Tips

- **Open an HSA the same year you enroll in a qualifying HDHP** — you cannot contribute retroactively, so don't wait.
- **Invest your HSA rather than spending it down** if you can afford to pay current medical expenses out of pocket — the investment growth compounds tax-free indefinitely.
- **Save HSA receipts permanently** — there's no time limit on reimbursing yourself for eligible expenses, meaning you can accumulate receipts and take reimbursements tax-free years later.
- **Don't conflate FSA rules with HSA rules** — flexible spending accounts have different rules and a use-it-or-lose-it feature. HSAs carry over indefinitely and are more flexible.
- **Ask your DPC practice for itemized receipts** for any services that may be HSA-eligible — labs, medications, procedures — so you have proper documentation.
- **Track HSA contributions against the annual limit** — excess contributions are subject to tax and penalty.
- **Stay informed on legislative changes** — the DPC-HSA legislation landscape is evolving, and what's not eligible today may become eligible in the future.

## FAQ

**Q: Why can't DPC membership fees be paid with an HSA?**
A: Under IRS Notice 2004-23 and related guidance, DPC membership fees are generally not considered "qualified medical expenses" because they function more like a prepaid health plan than a direct fee for a specific medical service. The IRS draws a distinction between paying for a specific medical service and paying for the right to access future services — and DPC memberships are classified as the latter.

**Q: Can I use a Flexible Spending Account (FSA) for DPC membership fees?**
A: The same basic analysis applies to FSAs as to HSAs — DPC membership fees are generally not considered qualified expenses under IRS rules. However, individual FSA plans may have different rules depending on how the plan administrator interprets the guidelines. Check with your FSA plan administrator directly.

**Q: Does having a DPC membership affect my ability to contribute to an HSA?**
A: Having a DPC membership itself does not disqualify you from contributing to an HSA. The key requirement is that you're enrolled in a qualifying high-deductible health plan (HDHP). If you have an HDHP, you can contribute to an HSA regardless of whether you also have a DPC membership. (There was some early debate about whether DPC membership might disqualify HSA eligibility, but regulatory guidance has clarified that DPC membership alone does not affect HSA eligibility.)

**Q: If Congress passes DPC-HSA legislation, will I be able to retroactively reimburse myself for past DPC membership fees?**
A: Probably not — HSA reimbursements are generally limited to expenses incurred after the account was opened and during the year for which the funds were contributed. If legislation changes the eligibility rules, it would likely apply prospectively (going forward), not retroactively. Consult a tax advisor if and when such legislation passes.
