Managing Chronic Conditions with Direct Primary Care: A Better Approach
Chronic conditions like diabetes and hypertension need regular attention, not 10-minute visits. DPC's longer appointments and direct access make real disease management possible.

## Quick Answer
Chronic conditions like diabetes, hypertension, and depression improve with consistent, attentive primary care — exactly what traditional rushed 10-minute visits can't provide. DPC's longer appointments, direct physician access, and proactive follow-up create the conditions chronic disease management actually requires.

## Explanation

Nearly half of American adults have at least one chronic condition. For many, managing that condition is a constant background task — medications to take, labs to monitor, lifestyle changes to maintain, symptoms to watch for. The frustrating reality is that traditional primary care, built around brief episodic visits, handles chronic disease poorly.

If you're managing diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, obesity, or depression in a traditional practice, you know what it feels like: a once-a-year physical where your doctor quickly reviews your labs, adjusts a medication, prints some educational materials you've already read, and sends you on your way. Between visits, your questions go to a nurse line. Your medication isn't working quite right but your next appointment is two months out.

Direct primary care is structured differently in ways that matter deeply for chronic disease.

### Why Traditional Care Fails Chronic Disease Patients

The problems stem directly from the economics of fee-for-service care:

**Visit frequency:** A physician with 2,500 patients cannot proactively reach out to patients with poorly controlled diabetes or untreated hypertension — there simply isn't enough time in the schedule. Patients are responsible for initiating care, and many don't until something goes wrong.

**Visit quality:** Ten minutes is not enough time to address medication side effects, review glucose trends, discuss dietary changes, address the depression that's making diabetes management harder, and answer questions — which is what a good diabetes visit actually requires. Physicians pick the most urgent issue and defer the rest.

**In-between access:** When a patient's blood pressure is reading high at home, or their blood sugar has been elevated for two weeks, they have limited options: call and wait for a callback, schedule an appointment weeks out, or go to urgent care. None of these are efficient or satisfying.

**Fragmented care:** People with multiple chronic conditions may see three or four specialists plus a primary care physician, with limited communication among them. The primary care physician who could coordinate that care doesn't have time to be a care coordinator at 25 patients per day.

### How DPC Changes the Equation

DPC practices are structured to do chronic disease management well:

**Longer visits:** A 1-to-2-hour appointment for diabetes management means your physician can review your glucose logs, examine your feet, discuss your A1c trends, adjust your medications, talk about what's working and what isn't, and address the emotional dimension of managing a lifelong condition. That's medicine as it should be practiced.

**Frequent check-ins:** DPC physicians can schedule monthly or bimonthly follow-ups for patients with poorly controlled conditions without worrying about filling appointment slots with higher-revenue visits. The membership model means follow-up is in everyone's interest.

**Direct messaging:** When your blood pressure has been elevated at home for a week, you text your physician. They respond. You might adjust your medication or get a telehealth visit — without waiting weeks for an appointment. This real-time responsiveness is genuinely important for chronic disease.

**Proactive outreach:** A DPC physician with 500 patients can actually know which patients are overdue for labs or follow-up and reach out to them. A physician with 2,500 patients cannot.

**Lifestyle medicine integration:** At Forward Family Medicine, board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine is integrated directly into chronic disease management — not as an add-on service, but as part of how we practice. Dr. Lieberman holds dual board eligibility in Family Medicine and Lifestyle Medicine and brings a background in culinary arts and nutrition science to every patient encounter. This means nutrition, physical activity, sleep, and stress are treated as first-line interventions alongside any medications — addressing the root causes of conditions like diabetes, hypertension, and metabolic syndrome, not just their downstream numbers. This is the only DPC practice on the Main Line offering this level of lifestyle medicine in-person and included in every membership.

### Conditions DPC Is Particularly Strong For

- **Type 2 diabetes** — requires ongoing monitoring, medication management, lifestyle coaching, and early complication detection. All of this benefits enormously from frequent, unhurried primary care contact.
- **Hypertension** — often needs medication titration over months, home monitoring follow-up, and lifestyle guidance. DPC's direct access is ideal for this iterative process.
- **Hyperlipidemia** — regular lab review, medication adjustment, and dietary conversation fit well in DPC's longer visit model.
- **Obesity and metabolic syndrome** — lifestyle medicine and weight management counseling require time and continuity that traditional care rarely provides.
- **Depression and anxiety** — mental health in primary care is dramatically better with a physician who knows you, has time to talk, and can follow your progress closely.
- **Hypothyroidism** — regular lab monitoring and medication adjustment are straightforward in DPC's unhurried model.
- **Asthma and COPD** — medication management, inhaler technique, and exacerbation prevention benefit from a physician who's proactively engaged.

### Specialist Coordination

DPC doesn't mean going it alone on complex cases. Your DPC physician should be an active coordinator with any specialists involved in your care — receiving notes, following up on recommendations, reconciling medications, and ensuring nothing falls through the cracks. In a well-functioning DPC practice, your primary care doctor is the quarterback of your care, not a passive referral generator.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

Chronic disease management is one of the areas where DPC's advantages are clearest, and it's an area we take seriously at Forward Family Medicine. Our physicians have time to do this work right — to sit with patients, understand the full picture, and develop a management plan that accounts for the whole person, not just the lab values.

We incorporate [lifestyle medicine](/lifestyle-medicine) directly into our chronic disease management approach, addressing the upstream factors — nutrition, physical activity, sleep, stress — that drive so many chronic conditions. This isn't a separate program; it's part of how we practice.

For patients managing conditions like diabetes, hypertension, depression, or metabolic syndrome, we offer the combination of thoughtful medical management, real access between visits, and genuine continuity that chronic disease requires.

Learn more about our [direct primary care approach](/direct-primary-care) or explore [membership options](/membership) to see how Forward Family Medicine can improve the management of your chronic condition.

## Practical Tips

- **Track your numbers at home** — blood pressure, blood sugar, weight — and share trends with your physician between visits. DPC makes this loop actually work.
- **Schedule proactive follow-ups** rather than waiting until something goes wrong. Regular check-ins allow for medication adjustments before a condition becomes poorly controlled.
- **Don't separate physical and mental health** — depression significantly worsens diabetes and hypertension outcomes. Talk to your DPC physician about both.
- **Ask about wholesale medications** — many chronic disease medications (metformin, lisinopril, statins, thyroid medications) are available at near-generic wholesale pricing through DPC practices, potentially saving hundreds per year.
- **Use telehealth for routine check-ins** — medication refills, lab reviews, blood pressure follow-ups often don't require an in-person visit. DPC makes these quick and easy.
- **Bring your medication list to every visit** — your DPC physician should know everything you're taking, including supplements and over-the-counter medications, to manage interactions and polypharmacy.
- **Ask your DPC physician to communicate with your specialists** — don't assume that happens automatically. A proactive DPC physician will facilitate this; make sure yours is doing so.

## FAQ

**Q: Can a DPC physician manage all of my chronic conditions, or will I still need specialists?**
A: Primary care physicians, including DPC doctors, can manage the vast majority of common chronic conditions — diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism, depression, and many others. Complex or poorly controlled conditions may benefit from specialist involvement, but your DPC physician can often manage more than a traditional rushed primary care visit would allow. When specialist referrals are needed, your DPC physician can coordinate that care actively.

**Q: Will my DPC physician have time to address multiple chronic conditions in one visit?**
A: Yes — this is one of the core differences. A 1-to-2-hour DPC visit is designed to accommodate complex patients with multiple conditions. Rather than triaging to the single most pressing issue, your physician can review your full picture. For very complex patients, some DPC practices schedule extended visits or series of focused visits to work through each condition systematically.

**Q: How does DPC handle the ongoing lab monitoring that many chronic conditions require?**
A: Most DPC practices offer labs at dramatically reduced prices — often 70–90% less than standard retail lab rates — through direct arrangements with lab companies. This makes regular monitoring more accessible and less expensive. Results are reviewed directly by your physician, who can message you with interpretation and next steps rather than routing you through a portal with no explanation.

**Q: I've had my chronic condition poorly controlled for years. Is DPC realistic for me?**
A: Yes, and frankly, patients with poorly controlled chronic conditions often benefit most from DPC. The additional access, time, and physician attention that DPC provides are exactly what's needed to make real progress on a condition that hasn't responded to the episodic care of a traditional practice. Many DPC physicians find this kind of patient particularly rewarding to work with.

Quick Answer

Chronic conditions like diabetes, hypertension, and depression improve with consistent, attentive primary care — exactly what traditional rushed 10-minute visits can’t provide. DPC’s longer appointments, direct physician access, and proactive follow-up create the conditions chronic disease management actually requires.

Explanation

Nearly half of American adults have at least one chronic condition. For many, managing that condition is a constant background task — medications to take, labs to monitor, lifestyle changes to maintain, symptoms to watch for. The frustrating reality is that traditional primary care, built around brief episodic visits, handles chronic disease poorly.

If you’re managing diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, obesity, or depression in a traditional practice, you know what it feels like: a once-a-year physical where your doctor quickly reviews your labs, adjusts a medication, prints some educational materials you’ve already read, and sends you on your way. Between visits, your questions go to a nurse line. Your medication isn’t working quite right but your next appointment is two months out.

Direct primary care is structured differently in ways that matter deeply for chronic disease.

Why Traditional Care Fails Chronic Disease Patients

The problems stem directly from the economics of fee-for-service care:

Visit frequency: A physician with 2,500 patients cannot proactively reach out to patients with poorly controlled diabetes or untreated hypertension — there simply isn’t enough time in the schedule. Patients are responsible for initiating care, and many don’t until something goes wrong.

Visit quality: Ten minutes is not enough time to address medication side effects, review glucose trends, discuss dietary changes, address the depression that’s making diabetes management harder, and answer questions — which is what a good diabetes visit actually requires. Physicians pick the most urgent issue and defer the rest.

In-between access: When a patient’s blood pressure is reading high at home, or their blood sugar has been elevated for two weeks, they have limited options: call and wait for a callback, schedule an appointment weeks out, or go to urgent care. None of these are efficient or satisfying.

Fragmented care: People with multiple chronic conditions may see three or four specialists plus a primary care physician, with limited communication among them. The primary care physician who could coordinate that care doesn’t have time to be a care coordinator at 25 patients per day.

How DPC Changes the Equation

DPC practices are structured to do chronic disease management well:

Longer visits: A 1-to-2-hour appointment for diabetes management means your physician can review your glucose logs, examine your feet, discuss your A1c trends, adjust your medications, talk about what’s working and what isn’t, and address the emotional dimension of managing a lifelong condition. That’s medicine as it should be practiced.

Frequent check-ins: DPC physicians can schedule monthly or bimonthly follow-ups for patients with poorly controlled conditions without worrying about filling appointment slots with higher-revenue visits. The membership model means follow-up is in everyone’s interest.

Direct messaging: When your blood pressure has been elevated at home for a week, you text your physician. They respond. You might adjust your medication or get a telehealth visit — without waiting weeks for an appointment. This real-time responsiveness is genuinely important for chronic disease.

Proactive outreach: A DPC physician with 500 patients can actually know which patients are overdue for labs or follow-up and reach out to them. A physician with 2,500 patients cannot.

Lifestyle medicine integration: At Forward Family Medicine, board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine is integrated directly into chronic disease management — not as an add-on service, but as part of how we practice. Dr. Lieberman holds dual board eligibility in Family Medicine and Lifestyle Medicine and brings a background in culinary arts and nutrition science to every patient encounter. This means nutrition, physical activity, sleep, and stress are treated as first-line interventions alongside any medications — addressing the root causes of conditions like diabetes, hypertension, and metabolic syndrome, not just their downstream numbers. This is the only DPC practice on the Main Line offering this level of lifestyle medicine in-person and included in every membership.

Conditions DPC Is Particularly Strong For

  • Type 2 diabetes — requires ongoing monitoring, medication management, lifestyle coaching, and early complication detection. All of this benefits enormously from frequent, unhurried primary care contact.
  • Hypertension — often needs medication titration over months, home monitoring follow-up, and lifestyle guidance. DPC’s direct access is ideal for this iterative process.
  • Hyperlipidemia — regular lab review, medication adjustment, and dietary conversation fit well in DPC’s longer visit model.
  • Obesity and metabolic syndrome — lifestyle medicine and weight management counseling require time and continuity that traditional care rarely provides.
  • Depression and anxiety — mental health in primary care is dramatically better with a physician who knows you, has time to talk, and can follow your progress closely.
  • Hypothyroidism — regular lab monitoring and medication adjustment are straightforward in DPC’s unhurried model.
  • Asthma and COPD — medication management, inhaler technique, and exacerbation prevention benefit from a physician who’s proactively engaged.

Specialist Coordination

DPC doesn’t mean going it alone on complex cases. Your DPC physician should be an active coordinator with any specialists involved in your care — receiving notes, following up on recommendations, reconciling medications, and ensuring nothing falls through the cracks. In a well-functioning DPC practice, your primary care doctor is the quarterback of your care, not a passive referral generator.

How Forward Family Medicine Helps

Chronic disease management is one of the areas where DPC’s advantages are clearest, and it’s an area we take seriously at Forward Family Medicine. Our physicians have time to do this work right — to sit with patients, understand the full picture, and develop a management plan that accounts for the whole person, not just the lab values.

We incorporate lifestyle medicine directly into our chronic disease management approach, addressing the upstream factors — nutrition, physical activity, sleep, stress — that drive so many chronic conditions. This isn’t a separate program; it’s part of how we practice.

For patients managing conditions like diabetes, hypertension, depression, or metabolic syndrome, we offer the combination of thoughtful medical management, real access between visits, and genuine continuity that chronic disease requires.

Learn more about our direct primary care approach or explore membership options to see how Forward Family Medicine can improve the management of your chronic condition.

Practical Tips

  • Track your numbers at home — blood pressure, blood sugar, weight — and share trends with your physician between visits. DPC makes this loop actually work.
  • Schedule proactive follow-ups rather than waiting until something goes wrong. Regular check-ins allow for medication adjustments before a condition becomes poorly controlled.
  • Don’t separate physical and mental health — depression significantly worsens diabetes and hypertension outcomes. Talk to your DPC physician about both.
  • Ask about wholesale medications — many chronic disease medications (metformin, lisinopril, statins, thyroid medications) are available at near-generic wholesale pricing through DPC practices, potentially saving hundreds per year.
  • Use telehealth for routine check-ins — medication refills, lab reviews, blood pressure follow-ups often don’t require an in-person visit. DPC makes these quick and easy.
  • Bring your medication list to every visit — your DPC physician should know everything you’re taking, including supplements and over-the-counter medications, to manage interactions and polypharmacy.
  • Ask your DPC physician to communicate with your specialists — don’t assume that happens automatically. A proactive DPC physician will facilitate this; make sure yours is doing so.

FAQ

Q: Can a DPC physician manage all of my chronic conditions, or will I still need specialists? A: Primary care physicians, including DPC doctors, can manage the vast majority of common chronic conditions — diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism, depression, and many others. Complex or poorly controlled conditions may benefit from specialist involvement, but your DPC physician can often manage more than a traditional rushed primary care visit would allow. When specialist referrals are needed, your DPC physician can coordinate that care actively.

Q: Will my DPC physician have time to address multiple chronic conditions in one visit? A: Yes — this is one of the core differences. A 1-to-2-hour DPC visit is designed to accommodate complex patients with multiple conditions. Rather than triaging to the single most pressing issue, your physician can review your full picture. For very complex patients, some DPC practices schedule extended visits or series of focused visits to work through each condition systematically.

Q: How does DPC handle the ongoing lab monitoring that many chronic conditions require? A: Most DPC practices offer labs at dramatically reduced prices — often 70–90% less than standard retail lab rates — through direct arrangements with lab companies. This makes regular monitoring more accessible and less expensive. Results are reviewed directly by your physician, who can message you with interpretation and next steps rather than routing you through a portal with no explanation.

Q: I’ve had my chronic condition poorly controlled for years. Is DPC realistic for me? A: Yes, and frankly, patients with poorly controlled chronic conditions often benefit most from DPC. The additional access, time, and physician attention that DPC provides are exactly what’s needed to make real progress on a condition that hasn’t responded to the episodic care of a traditional practice. Many DPC physicians find this kind of patient particularly rewarding to work with.

Direct Primary Care for Families: Is It Worth It?
DPC membership for families means one doctor who knows everyone, no per-visit fees for sick kids, and real savings over copays and deductibles. Here's how it works.

## Quick Answer
Direct primary care can be an excellent fit for families — per-member pricing, no per-visit fees, and one physician who knows your whole family means fewer surprises and better care for kids and adults alike. For families who use primary care regularly, the math often favors DPC over traditional copays and deductibles.

## Explanation

When you have kids, primary care isn't something you use occasionally — it's something you use constantly. Ear infections, sports physicals, back-to-school checkups, mystery rashes at 7 p.m. on a Friday, follow-ups after strep. Add adults managing their own health needs and the cumulative cost of traditional primary care in a family of four can be staggering before you've even thought about a specialist.

Direct primary care offers a different model, and for families, the fit can be especially good.

### How DPC Pricing Works for Families

Most DPC practices charge on a per-member basis, with pricing that typically varies by age:

- **Children** are usually the least expensive tier — often significantly less than adult pricing, reflecting that their care needs, while frequent, tend to be shorter and more straightforward.
- **Adults** pay a standard monthly rate, often with slight increases at older age brackets.
- **Families** pay the sum of their individual member rates, with no per-visit fees on top.

This structure means a family of four pays a predictable monthly amount and can see the doctor as many times as needed throughout the month with no additional cost. Contrast that with a family carrying a traditional PPO — $30–$50 in copays per visit adds up fast when you have two kids who need to be seen four to six times per year each, plus adult visits on top.

Visit our [membership page](/membership) for current family pricing at Forward Family Medicine.

### The Value of One Physician Who Knows Everyone

One of the most underappreciated benefits of family DPC isn't the cost — it's the continuity. When one physician follows your whole family over time, they accumulate something genuinely valuable: context.

Your doctor knows that your youngest tends to get ear infections that resolve on their own. They know your teenager has anxiety that surfaces as stomachaches during the school year. They know your spouse has a family history of early heart disease and takes that into account when reviewing labs. They know you've been struggling to sleep.

In a traditional practice, that continuity is frequently broken. You might see a different provider each time because availability is limited, or your children's pediatrician is across town from your own internist, and no one is looking at the full picture. DPC restores that coherence.

### Is DPC Appropriate for Children?

Absolutely. Many DPC physicians practice family medicine, meaning they're trained to care for patients from infancy through adulthood. Well-child visits, immunizations, developmental screenings, sports physicals, and acute sick care are all well within the scope of DPC.

A few things worth knowing:

- **Immunizations** at DPC practices may be handled differently than at a traditional pediatric office. Some DPC practices stock vaccines as part of membership; others have patients obtain vaccines through their pharmacy or county health department. Ask your DPC practice how they handle this before enrolling.
- **Newborns and infants** can typically be enrolled in DPC from birth.
- **Adolescents** often benefit enormously from having a trusted physician relationship — particularly for mental health conversations, which tend to go better when there's an established relationship rather than a rushed visit with a stranger.

### Lifestyle Medicine for the Whole Family

One additional benefit worth highlighting for families at Forward Family Medicine: integrated board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine. Dr. Lieberman's background in culinary arts, nutrition science, and Lifestyle Medicine means the whole family has access to evidence-based nutritional guidance, metabolic health support, and lifestyle coaching — not as a referral, but built into your membership. For parents wanting to build healthy habits for their children, or adults managing their own metabolic health, this is a clinical resource most DPC practices simply don't offer.

### The Real Cost Comparison

Let's sketch out a rough comparison for a family of four — two adults, two kids — currently on a PPO:

**Traditional PPO:**
- Monthly premium: $800–$1,200/month (employer may subsidize, but many families contribute $400–$600/month)
- Primary care copays: $30–$50 each, 8–12 visits/year = $240–$600/year
- Specialist copays if needed: additional cost
- Deductible exposure: $1,500–$3,000 per person if anyone gets sick

**DPC + HDHP:**
- DPC membership for family of four: varies by practice (visit [membership page](/membership))
- HDHP premium: often $300–$500/month less than comparable PPO
- Primary care visits: no additional cost (included in membership)
- HSA contributions possible to cover deductible exposure — and following the Primary Care Enhancement Act (effective January 1, 2026), DPC membership fees are now HSA-eligible when paired with a qualifying HDHP — an additional financial benefit worth factoring into your total cost comparison

For families who regularly use primary care, the DPC + HDHP combination frequently delivers equivalent or better care at a meaningfully lower total annual spend.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

At Forward Family Medicine, we love caring for families. Knowing the whole family — watching kids grow up, understanding family health patterns, being the physician your family actually trusts — is exactly why we went into medicine.

Our practice serves families across Wayne and the Main Line area. We keep our patient panel small on purpose, so when your child is sick on a Monday morning, you can reach us. When your teenager needs to talk to a doctor, we have time for that conversation. When you have a question about your spouse's labs, you can text us rather than scheduling another appointment.

We offer per-member family pricing designed to make DPC accessible for households with multiple members. Learn more on our [membership page](/membership), or visit our [direct primary care overview](/direct-primary-care) to understand what the model includes.

## Practical Tips

- **Enroll the whole family at once** — the continuity benefit compounds when one physician knows everyone.
- **Use sick visits liberally** — you're already paying the monthly fee; don't default to urgent care when your child has an ear infection. Call your DPC practice first.
- **Ask about vaccine logistics upfront** — before enrolling, confirm how your DPC practice handles childhood immunizations to avoid surprises.
- **Use telehealth for quick questions** — rashes, pink eye, post-strep follow-ups, and minor concerns can often be handled by video or photo, saving everyone a trip to the office.
- **Schedule well-child visits early in the year** — DPC practices can usually accommodate these quickly, but getting ahead of the back-to-school rush makes sense.
- **Have a conversation with your doctor about the family's health patterns** — a good DPC physician will want to know about family history, recurring issues, and any concerns you have about each family member.
- **Compare your actual annual spend** — add up last year's premiums, copays, deductibles, and urgent care visits to get a real number before dismissing DPC as "too expensive."

## FAQ

**Q: Can a family doctor in a DPC practice see my newborn?**
A: Most DPC family medicine physicians can care for newborns and infants. You'll want to confirm before enrolling, as some practices have age minimums. Newborn care in a DPC setting can be wonderful — your physician already knows your family and can provide consistent, personalized attention from the very start.

**Q: What if my child needs a specialist?**
A: DPC covers primary care. If your child needs a specialist, that referral goes through your health insurance as it would with any primary care physician. A good DPC physician will help coordinate that referral, communicate with the specialist, and follow up afterward — which is often better coordination than families receive in traditional care.

**Q: Are sports physicals and school forms included in the DPC membership?**
A: In most DPC practices, yes — sports physicals, school physical forms, and camp forms are included in membership and are not billed separately. This is a meaningful benefit for families with school-age children who need these documents annually.

**Q: Is DPC worth it if my family is generally healthy and rarely needs the doctor?**
A: It depends on how you look at it. If your family truly uses primary care once every couple of years and has good insurance with low copays, DPC may not deliver obvious cost savings. But many families find that when they finally have easy access to a doctor they trust — no waiting, no hassle — they actually engage with preventive care more, catch issues earlier, and feel less anxious about their health. The value isn't always purely financial.

Direct Primary Care for the Self-Employed: The Healthcare Solution You've Been Missing
Self-employed? DPC paired with a high-deductible plan gives you excellent primary care, lower overall costs, and no dependence on an employer for healthcare access.

## Quick Answer
For freelancers, 1099 workers, and small business owners, DPC paired with a high-deductible health plan delivers excellent primary care at a predictable monthly cost — often cheaper than ACA premiums alone, without the gaps in access that come with high-deductible coverage.

## Explanation

If you're self-employed, healthcare is probably one of the most frustrating parts of your financial life. You're paying full ACA marketplace premiums out of pocket. Your deductible may be $3,000, $5,000, or more. You're essentially self-insuring for most routine care — and when you actually need to see a doctor, the process is slow, inconvenient, and expensive.

This is the healthcare paradox of the self-employed: you pay a lot for coverage that doesn't serve you well for everyday needs.

Direct primary care doesn't solve everything, but it solves the part of the healthcare system that you interact with most: your relationship with a primary care doctor.

### The Problem with ACA Plans for 1099 Workers

The Affordable Care Act marketplace was a meaningful improvement for people who had no coverage at all. But for self-employed individuals earning a decent income, ACA plans often mean paying substantial premiums for plans with high deductibles — meaning you pay out of pocket for most primary care anyway.

The math goes something like this: you pay $400–$800/month in premiums (depending on your age, location, and income). You have a $4,000 deductible. Your primary care visit costs $150–$200 out of pocket until you meet that deductible — which may never happen in a healthy year. You're paying a lot for catastrophic protection and getting very little in return for routine care.

### How the DPC + HDHP Strategy Changes the Math

The smarter approach for many self-employed individuals:

1. **Enroll in DPC** for primary care. Pay a flat monthly membership fee — visit our [membership page](/membership) for current pricing at Forward Family Medicine. Get unlimited primary care, same-day access, telehealth, and often wholesale labs and medications.

2. **Pair with a high-deductible health plan (HDHP)** for catastrophic coverage. HDHPs carry the lowest monthly premiums of any ACA-compliant plan. You're protected against major events — hospitalization, surgery, specialist care — without paying for benefits you're not using at the primary care level.

3. **Open a health savings account (HSA)** to shelter pre-tax dollars for medical expenses. Following the Primary Care Enhancement Act (effective January 1, 2026), DPC membership fees are now HSA-eligible when paired with a qualifying HDHP — meaning your monthly membership fee can be paid with pre-tax HSA dollars. Labs, medications, and procedures continue to be HSA-eligible as before.

For many self-employed individuals, the total annual spend — DPC membership + HDHP premium — is less than what they were paying for a mid-tier ACA plan while also receiving dramatically better primary care.

### The Hidden Cost of Not Having Good Primary Care Access

There's another dimension to this that goes beyond premiums: what does poor healthcare access actually cost you?

When you're self-employed, your time is money. Spending two hours at urgent care for a sinus infection because you can't get a same-day appointment with your doctor costs you in lost productivity, not just a $200 urgent care bill. Letting a manageable chronic condition drift because follow-up appointments are too hard to get means bigger, more expensive problems down the road.

DPC membership gives you something genuinely different: a doctor you can text. One who knows your health history and will respond without requiring you to schedule an appointment three weeks out. One who can handle a quick question in five minutes instead of requiring a full visit.

For a self-employed person whose time is genuinely valuable, that access is worth real money.

### Who Benefits Most

DPC + HDHP is particularly compelling for:

- **Solo freelancers and consultants** who buy insurance on the individual market
- **Self-employed individuals with chronic conditions** like diabetes, hypertension, or hypothyroidism who need regular primary care contact — and who want a physician who treats root causes, not just numbers. At Forward Family Medicine, every membership includes integrated board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine with Dr. Lieberman at no additional cost. For a self-employed person paying out-of-pocket, getting clinical nutrition and lifestyle medicine included in your primary care membership — rather than paying separately for it — is a significant value difference.
- **Small business owners** who want a better healthcare benefit for themselves (and potentially their small team)
- **Couples where one or both partners are self-employed**
- **People who have historically deferred care** because traditional primary care felt too slow, too expensive, or too inaccessible

### What to Do About Coverage Gaps

One concern self-employed people sometimes raise: what if something major happens while I'm on an HDHP? The deductible feels scary.

The answer is your HSA. If you're contributing the IRS maximum to your HSA each year ($4,300 for individuals / $8,550 for families in 2026), you're building a tax-advantaged reserve that can cover your deductible if needed. Over a few years, a well-funded HSA gives you meaningful protection against deductible exposure while also growing tax-free.

Combined with DPC handling your routine care, the result is a healthcare strategy that's both financially smart and genuinely good for your health.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

We have a lot of self-employed members at Forward Family Medicine, and it makes sense — independent workers are exactly the people who understand the value of reliable, responsive service. You wouldn't accept a business vendor who took three weeks to return your call. Why accept that from your doctor?

As a DPC practice, we give self-employed patients the kind of access and attention that used to be reserved for people with premium employer health benefits. You can reach your physician directly. We return calls and messages promptly. When you need a same-day visit, we have time for you.

We also help members navigate the insurance side of the equation. If you're thinking about switching to an HDHP to pair with DPC, we're happy to talk through how that would work for your health situation.

Explore [our membership options](/membership) or learn more about how our [direct primary care model](/direct-primary-care) works for independent workers and small business owners.

## Practical Tips

- **Run the numbers** — add up what you paid last year in premiums, copays, deductibles, and urgent care before assuming DPC is more expensive.
- **Open an HSA if you're on an HDHP** — the triple tax benefit (contributions pre-tax, growth tax-free, withdrawals tax-free for medical) is the best healthcare tax strategy available to self-employed individuals. (Note: Under the Primary Care Enhancement Act effective January 1, 2026, DPC membership fees are now HSA-eligible — confirm with your plan administrator.)
- **Maximize your primary care usage** — if you're paying a monthly fee, use it. Annual physical, chronic condition follow-ups, mental health check-ins, telehealth questions — all included.
- **Factor in time** — urgent care waits, scheduling delays, and missed productivity all have real dollar values when you're self-employed.
- **Consider group DPC arrangements** — if you own a small business with employees, some DPC practices offer group pricing that can be a cost-effective benefit.
- **Don't wait until you're sick** — the real value of DPC is building a relationship with a physician who knows you. Enroll before you need it.
- **Check if your DPC practice offers medication dispensing** — wholesale drug pricing at a DPC practice can be a significant savings for common chronic condition medications.

## FAQ

**Q: Is DPC membership tax-deductible if I'm self-employed?**
A: This is a nuanced question. Self-employed individuals can generally deduct health insurance premiums, but the IRS has not clearly ruled that DPC membership fees qualify as a deductible healthcare expense in the same category. Some tax advisors treat DPC as a deductible medical expense; others are more conservative. Consult a CPA or tax advisor familiar with healthcare for the most current guidance.

**Q: Can I enroll in DPC without any health insurance at all?**
A: Technically yes — there is no legal requirement to carry health insurance alongside a DPC membership. However, it is strongly discouraged. DPC covers primary care only. Without insurance, a single hospitalization, emergency, or serious diagnosis could generate catastrophic debt. DPC is a complement to insurance, not a replacement for it.

**Q: What if I have a slow month and can't afford both DPC and my HDHP premium?**
A: This is a real concern for variable-income earners. Most DPC practices allow month-to-month membership with no long-term contract, though some offer modest discounts for annual payment. Your HDHP is the non-negotiable piece — keep that coverage. If you need to pause DPC temporarily during a lean stretch, most practices will allow you to re-enroll without penalty.

**Q: Does DPC help with specialist referrals and navigating the healthcare system?**
A: Absolutely — and this is one of the less-discussed benefits. A good DPC physician acts as a healthcare navigator for you. When you need a specialist, they help identify the right one, send a proper referral with your history, and follow up on the results. For self-employed individuals without HR departments to call for guidance, having a knowledgeable physician in your corner navigating the system is genuinely valuable.

Quick Answer

For freelancers, 1099 workers, and small business owners, DPC paired with a high-deductible health plan delivers excellent primary care at a predictable monthly cost — often cheaper than ACA premiums alone, without the gaps in access that come with high-deductible coverage.

Explanation

If you’re self-employed, healthcare is probably one of the most frustrating parts of your financial life. You’re paying full ACA marketplace premiums out of pocket. Your deductible may be $3,000, $5,000, or more. You’re essentially self-insuring for most routine care — and when you actually need to see a doctor, the process is slow, inconvenient, and expensive.

This is the healthcare paradox of the self-employed: you pay a lot for coverage that doesn’t serve you well for everyday needs.

Direct primary care doesn’t solve everything, but it solves the part of the healthcare system that you interact with most: your relationship with a primary care doctor.

The Problem with ACA Plans for 1099 Workers

The Affordable Care Act marketplace was a meaningful improvement for people who had no coverage at all. But for self-employed individuals earning a decent income, ACA plans often mean paying substantial premiums for plans with high deductibles — meaning you pay out of pocket for most primary care anyway.

The math goes something like this: you pay $400–$800/month in premiums (depending on your age, location, and income). You have a $4,000 deductible. Your primary care visit costs $150–$200 out of pocket until you meet that deductible — which may never happen in a healthy year. You’re paying a lot for catastrophic protection and getting very little in return for routine care.

How the DPC + HDHP Strategy Changes the Math

The smarter approach for many self-employed individuals:

  1. Enroll in DPC for primary care. Pay a flat monthly membership fee — visit our membership page for current pricing at Forward Family Medicine. Get unlimited primary care, same-day access, telehealth, and often wholesale labs and medications.

  2. Pair with a high-deductible health plan (HDHP) for catastrophic coverage. HDHPs carry the lowest monthly premiums of any ACA-compliant plan. You’re protected against major events — hospitalization, surgery, specialist care — without paying for benefits you’re not using at the primary care level.

  3. Open a health savings account (HSA) to shelter pre-tax dollars for medical expenses. Following the Primary Care Enhancement Act (effective January 1, 2026), DPC membership fees are now HSA-eligible when paired with a qualifying HDHP — meaning your monthly membership fee can be paid with pre-tax HSA dollars. Labs, medications, and procedures continue to be HSA-eligible as before.

For many self-employed individuals, the total annual spend — DPC membership + HDHP premium — is less than what they were paying for a mid-tier ACA plan while also receiving dramatically better primary care.

The Hidden Cost of Not Having Good Primary Care Access

There’s another dimension to this that goes beyond premiums: what does poor healthcare access actually cost you?

When you’re self-employed, your time is money. Spending two hours at urgent care for a sinus infection because you can’t get a same-day appointment with your doctor costs you in lost productivity, not just a $200 urgent care bill. Letting a manageable chronic condition drift because follow-up appointments are too hard to get means bigger, more expensive problems down the road.

DPC membership gives you something genuinely different: a doctor you can text. One who knows your health history and will respond without requiring you to schedule an appointment three weeks out. One who can handle a quick question in five minutes instead of requiring a full visit.

For a self-employed person whose time is genuinely valuable, that access is worth real money.

Who Benefits Most

DPC + HDHP is particularly compelling for:

  • Solo freelancers and consultants who buy insurance on the individual market
  • Self-employed individuals with chronic conditions like diabetes, hypertension, or hypothyroidism who need regular primary care contact — and who want a physician who treats root causes, not just numbers. At Forward Family Medicine, every membership includes integrated board-eligible Lifestyle Medicine with Dr. Lieberman at no additional cost. For a self-employed person paying out-of-pocket, getting clinical nutrition and lifestyle medicine included in your primary care membership — rather than paying separately for it — is a significant value difference.
  • Small business owners who want a better healthcare benefit for themselves (and potentially their small team)
  • Couples where one or both partners are self-employed
  • People who have historically deferred care because traditional primary care felt too slow, too expensive, or too inaccessible

What to Do About Coverage Gaps

One concern self-employed people sometimes raise: what if something major happens while I’m on an HDHP? The deductible feels scary.

The answer is your HSA. If you’re contributing the IRS maximum to your HSA each year ($4,300 for individuals / $8,550 for families in 2026), you’re building a tax-advantaged reserve that can cover your deductible if needed. Over a few years, a well-funded HSA gives you meaningful protection against deductible exposure while also growing tax-free.

Combined with DPC handling your routine care, the result is a healthcare strategy that’s both financially smart and genuinely good for your health.

How Forward Family Medicine Helps

We have a lot of self-employed members at Forward Family Medicine, and it makes sense — independent workers are exactly the people who understand the value of reliable, responsive service. You wouldn’t accept a business vendor who took three weeks to return your call. Why accept that from your doctor?

As a DPC practice, we give self-employed patients the kind of access and attention that used to be reserved for people with premium employer health benefits. You can reach your physician directly. We return calls and messages promptly. When you need a same-day visit, we have time for you.

We also help members navigate the insurance side of the equation. If you’re thinking about switching to an HDHP to pair with DPC, we’re happy to talk through how that would work for your health situation.

Explore our membership options or learn more about how our direct primary care model works for independent workers and small business owners.

Practical Tips

  • Run the numbers — add up what you paid last year in premiums, copays, deductibles, and urgent care before assuming DPC is more expensive.
  • Open an HSA if you’re on an HDHP — the triple tax benefit (contributions pre-tax, growth tax-free, withdrawals tax-free for medical) is the best healthcare tax strategy available to self-employed individuals. (Note: Under the Primary Care Enhancement Act effective January 1, 2026, DPC membership fees are now HSA-eligible — confirm with your plan administrator.)
  • Maximize your primary care usage — if you’re paying a monthly fee, use it. Annual physical, chronic condition follow-ups, mental health check-ins, telehealth questions — all included.
  • Factor in time — urgent care waits, scheduling delays, and missed productivity all have real dollar values when you’re self-employed.
  • Consider group DPC arrangements — if you own a small business with employees, some DPC practices offer group pricing that can be a cost-effective benefit.
  • Don’t wait until you’re sick — the real value of DPC is building a relationship with a physician who knows you. Enroll before you need it.
  • Check if your DPC practice offers medication dispensing — wholesale drug pricing at a DPC practice can be a significant savings for common chronic condition medications.

Why Your Doctor's Patient Panel Size Affects Your Care
Traditional doctors manage 2,000–3,000 patients each. DPC doctors cap at 400–600. That difference in panel size explains almost everything about why primary care feels broken.
how many patients does a doctor have

## Quick Answer
The average traditional primary care physician manages 2,000 to 3,000 patients — requiring 20 to 30 visits per day to stay financially viable. DPC physicians cap their panels at roughly 300–600 patients — already a fraction of the traditional 2,000–3,000. At Forward Family Medicine, we cap at approximately 250 patients per physician — smaller than the DPC average — because we believe that number is what genuine availability and true continuity actually require. Panel size is the root cause of most primary care frustrations.

## Explanation

Have you ever wondered why it takes three weeks to get a routine appointment with your primary care doctor? Or why your visit felt like it lasted exactly seven minutes? Or why you left with your questions only half-answered?

The answer isn't that your doctor doesn't care. It's almost certainly that your doctor is managing far too many patients.

### The Math Behind Traditional Primary Care

Traditional primary care physicians are paid through insurance reimbursement — a set fee for each billing code associated with each visit. To cover their overhead (staff, rent, malpractice insurance, billing software, electronic health records) and earn a living, a traditional primary care practice needs to see a certain number of patients every single day.

The numbers work out roughly like this:

- A typical primary care practice has operating costs of $500,000–$700,000 per year
- Average reimbursement per primary care visit runs $100–$150 after insurance contracts and write-offs
- To break even and earn a reasonable salary, a physician needs to see approximately 20–30 patients every working day
- At that volume, with 48 working weeks per year, that means a panel of 2,000–3,000 active patients

This is not a conspiracy or negligence — it is arithmetic. The fee-for-service system forces primary care physicians into a volume trap. See more patients, generate more revenue. The only alternative is to run a financially unsustainable practice.

### What 2,500 Patients Means for You

When your doctor has 2,500 patients in their panel, here is your practical reality:

**Access:** If each of those patients needs to be seen just once per year, that's 2,500 visits to fit into roughly 240 working days. That's already more than 10 per day for routine annual visits alone — before anyone gets sick. Getting a same-day appointment when you genuinely need to be seen requires bumping someone else or squeezing you into a gap that may not exist.

**Visit length:** If your doctor is seeing 25 patients in an eight-hour day, the math allows roughly 19 minutes per patient — and that includes charting, reviewing labs, and handling in-basket messages between visits. By the time the physician actually walks in the room, you may have 10–12 minutes of actual face time. Studies consistently find the average primary care visit in the U.S. runs 7–10 minutes.

**Continuity:** When someone in a 2,500-patient panel calls with a question, the doctor may not remember their history off the top of their head. The staff handles a large portion of triage. Your call may get routed to a nurse who answers your question without the physician ever being involved.

**Responsiveness:** After-hours questions go to a nurse line or prompt a trip to urgent care, because it's simply not possible for one physician to be truly available to 2,500 people.

### The DPC Model: What 500 Patients Changes

DPC practices generally cap their panels at roughly 300–600 patients. At Forward Family Medicine, our cap is approximately 250 patients per physician — below the DPC average — which is what makes same-day access consistently reliable, not just theoretically possible. The funding model is memberships rather than per-visit billing, which means the physician's income is stable without seeing a high daily volume.

The numbers work out very differently:

- With 500 patients and a full schedule, a DPC physician typically sees 10–15 patients per day
- That allows 1-to-2-hour appointments — time to actually talk, examine, review history, and answer questions
- Because visit volume is lower, same-day and next-day scheduling is structurally possible, not the exception
- After-hours contact is manageable because the patient panel is small enough to be actually known by the physician
- The physician can proactively reach out to patients who need follow-up, because they know who those patients are

This is not a philosophical difference in care philosophy — it is a structural difference. The panel cap is what makes everything else possible.

### Why This Matters for Outcomes

Research consistently shows that continuity of care with a primary care physician is associated with lower rates of hospitalization, fewer emergency department visits, better management of chronic conditions, and higher patient satisfaction. Those outcomes require a physician who knows you, has time for you, and is available when you need them.

None of those things are scalable at 2,500 patients per physician. They are entirely achievable at 500.

The panel size difference is, in a very real sense, the entire argument for direct primary care.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

At Forward Family Medicine, our panel is intentionally capped to ensure that every member receives the kind of care that's only possible when a physician genuinely knows their patients. We are not trying to maximize volume — we are trying to maximize care quality and availability.

When you are a member, your physician knows your name, your history, your family situation, and your health concerns before you walk in the door. When you call, you reach someone who knows your file. When you text, you get a thoughtful response, not a triage protocol.

That level of care is made possible specifically because we keep our panel small. It is the foundational commitment behind the [direct primary care model](/direct-primary-care) we practice.

If you'd like to experience primary care the way it should work, we invite you to explore our [membership options](/membership). Spots are limited — precisely because we believe that a smaller panel makes for better medicine.

## Practical Tips

- **Ask your current doctor their panel size** — most practices won't advertise this, but it's a legitimate question that tells you a lot about your likely experience.
- **Notice the access signals** — if it takes more than 2–3 business days to get a sick visit, that's a panel size problem, not a scheduling problem.
- **Value visit length** — a doctor who can spend 1 to 2 hours with you for a chronic disease review will provide genuinely better care than one with 10 minutes. That difference matters.
- **Understand that your doctor's frustration is real too** — physicians in high-volume practices often want to practice differently but are trapped by the economics of fee-for-service.
- **Think about the compound effect of continuity** — a physician who sees you consistently over years accumulates insight about your health that a new face every visit cannot replicate.
- **Don't assume DPC is only for the wealthy** — smaller panel + lower overhead + no billing staff often means DPC practices can operate at surprisingly accessible price points.
- **Ask about panel availability before enrolling** — good DPC practices close enrollment when they're full, because that's the whole point. Don't wait too long once you've decided.

## FAQ

**Q: Why don't traditional primary care practices just limit their panels?**
A: Because the fee-for-service payment model makes it financially impossible. If a traditional practice limited to 500 patients, they'd be seeing perhaps 8–12 patients per day at standard reimbursement rates — which wouldn't cover operating costs, let alone physician salary. The panel cap is only viable in DPC because membership fees replace per-visit billing and create a sustainable revenue model at lower volume.

**Q: Does a smaller panel mean a DPC practice earns less than a traditional practice?**
A: Not necessarily. DPC physicians trade high volume for predictable, stable membership revenue. Many DPC physicians report earning similar to or better than their fee-for-service peers — with less administrative burden, more satisfying work, and more control over their practice. The economic model is different, not inferior.

**Q: What happens when a DPC practice's panel fills up?**
A: Most DPC practices maintain a waitlist once they reach capacity. This is actually a feature, not a bug — it means the practice is committed to the panel limit rather than overriding it for revenue reasons. If you're interested in a DPC practice, it's worth joining a waitlist or enrolling before the panel closes.

**Q: Is a 400–600 patient panel actually enough to run a practice?**
A: Yes, when funded by membership fees. At $100–$200/month per adult member and 250–500 members, a DPC practice generates $300,000–$1.2M/year depending on panel size and membership rate. With leaner overhead — no billing staff, no insurance contracts — this supports a well-run practice delivering meaningfully better care than the fee-for-service model.

Telehealth in Direct Primary Care: How Virtual Visits Work
DPC telehealth isn't a stranger on a video call — it's your own physician who knows your history, included in your membership with no per-visit fee. Here's how it works.

## Quick Answer
Telehealth in DPC is fundamentally different from generic telehealth platforms — it's your own physician, who already knows your history, available to you by text, phone, or video as part of your membership with no per-visit fee. It's the convenience of virtual care with the quality of a real relationship.

## Explanation

Telehealth exploded during the pandemic, and many patients discovered that virtual visits could be genuinely useful. But most commercial telehealth platforms have a significant limitation: you're connecting with a random physician who has never met you, doesn't know your history, and will likely never see you again.

That physician follows a protocol. They're cautious because they don't know you. They prescribe conservatively, recommend you follow up with your "regular doctor," and the interaction ends when the video call does.

Telehealth in a DPC practice is something different.

### What Makes DPC Telehealth Different

When you have a video call, phone call, or text exchange with your DPC physician, the conversation begins with a foundation that commercial telehealth can never replicate: your doctor already knows you.

They know your medical history, your medication list, your allergies, your anxiety about certain symptoms, your family history, the chronic condition you've been managing, and the pattern of how you typically present when you're sick. That context changes everything.

A physician who knows you can:

- **Make faster, more confident decisions** — they're not hedging because they lack context
- **Give you a real assessment instead of a defensive one** — they know whether your symptoms fit a concerning pattern or a familiar benign one
- **Follow up** — a DPC telehealth visit doesn't end the relationship. Your physician will check in, review test results, and continue the conversation
- **Integrate the telehealth visit into your ongoing care** — what happened during your telehealth visit is part of your record and informs future care

### How DPC Telehealth Access Works

Different DPC practices structure telehealth access differently, but most offer some combination of:

**Text messaging:** For quick questions, photo review of a rash or wound, medication clarifications, or results questions, texting your physician directly is often the fastest path. This isn't about firing off texts at midnight for non-urgent questions — it's about having a professional communication channel that actually works.

**Phone calls:** For more nuanced conversations, a direct call with your physician is faster and more informative than the traditional "request a callback" approach. In most DPC practices, you're reaching your physician directly, not a nurse or answering service.

**Video visits:** Full video appointments are available for clinical assessments that benefit from visual examination — skin conditions, eye issues, observing how a patient looks and moves, conversations requiring nuance. Video works especially well for mental health check-ins, medication management, chronic disease follow-up, and second opinions on whether an in-person visit is needed.

### What Telehealth Handles Well in DPC

Many conditions and situations that generate in-person visits can be handled efficiently via telehealth:

- Upper respiratory infections and flu assessment
- UTI symptoms with prescription (where clinically appropriate)
- Rashes and skin conditions (photo-based assessment)
- Pink eye
- Medication questions, side effects, and refills
- Lab result review and interpretation
- Mental health check-ins and medication management for anxiety and depression
- Post-procedure or post-illness follow-up
- Blood pressure or blood sugar review after home monitoring
- Travel medicine consultations
- Pre-visit preparation for upcoming specialist appointments

### When In-Person Is Still Needed

Telehealth has real limits. Your DPC physician will ask you to come in when:

- A physical examination is clinically necessary (abdominal pain, chest symptoms, joint injuries, anything requiring hands-on assessment)
- Diagnostic testing needs to happen in the office (rapid strep or flu, urinalysis, EKG)
- A concern warrants more careful observation than a video call allows
- Immunizations, wound care, or minor procedures are needed

A good DPC physician will triage this honestly. They'll tell you when they can handle something remotely and when they need to see you. Their goal is efficient, high-quality care — not driving unnecessary in-person visits.

### DPC Telehealth vs. Commercial Telehealth Platforms

| | Commercial Telehealth (Teladoc, MDLive, etc.) | DPC Telehealth |
|---|---|---|
| **Who you see** | Random physician | Your own doctor |
| **Your history** | Unknown | Fully known |
| **Cost** | $75–$150 per visit | Included in membership |
| **Follow-up** | None | Integrated into ongoing care |
| **Decision-making** | Protocol-driven, conservative | Personalized, contextual |
| **Availability** | 24/7 for acute needs | Typically same-day; after-hours varies |
| **Continuity** | None | Complete |

### The Value for Busy Main Line Patients

For patients in Wayne, PA and the surrounding Main Line area, telehealth access through a DPC practice offers something particularly practical: primary care that fits into a busy life. A quick video call during your lunch break for a medication question. A text to your physician about your child's fever on a school morning. A follow-up call after your latest lab results without taking time off work for an office visit.

DPC telehealth doesn't replace in-person care. But it dramatically reduces the friction involved in staying connected with your physician — which leads to better care, more consistent management of chronic conditions, and a healthier relationship with your own healthcare.

## How Forward Family Medicine Helps

Telehealth visits are included in every Forward Family Medicine membership at no additional cost — the same direct access to your physician, no extra fee, no scheduling friction. Phone, video, and secure message access are built into the model.

At Forward Family Medicine, telehealth isn't a crisis-mode workaround — it's a regular part of how we practice. Our members can reach us by text, phone, or video for same-day triage and care. We use telehealth for follow-ups, medication management, lab reviews, and acute illness assessment, and we integrate everything into your ongoing care record.

When you join our practice, you get a real physician who knows you — not an algorithm or a stranger on a screen. That relationship is what makes the virtual part of our care actually work.

Learn more about how DPC works at our practice on our [direct primary care overview page](/direct-primary-care), or visit our [membership page](/membership) to see what's included in your membership.

## Practical Tips

- **Save your physician's contact information immediately** after enrolling — you don't want to be searching for it when you're sick.
- **Use text for non-urgent questions** — medication questions, photo of a rash, quick follow-up are all well-suited to messaging rather than scheduling a visit.
- **Take photos of skin conditions** in good lighting before your telehealth call — this dramatically improves the quality of a visual assessment.
- **Have your medication list ready** for telehealth calls, including any over-the-counter medications or supplements.
- **Use telehealth proactively for chronic condition management** — blood pressure trending up at home? Don't wait for an appointment. Reach out.
- **Ask your DPC practice about after-hours telehealth availability** so you know what to expect before you need it.
- **Trust your physician's judgment on in-person triage** — if they say come in, come in. If they say this can be handled remotely, trust that too.

## FAQ

**Q: Is DPC telehealth covered by insurance?**
A: In most DPC practices, telehealth visits are included in your membership — there's no separate charge, and nothing is billed to insurance. This is one of the concrete financial advantages of DPC: you don't have to think about cost before reaching out to your doctor.

**Q: Can my DPC physician prescribe medications via telehealth?**
A: Yes, in most cases. Your DPC physician can prescribe medications based on a telehealth assessment for many common conditions. Controlled substances (opioids, benzodiazepines, stimulants) have stricter telehealth prescribing rules under federal law and typically require an in-person evaluation at least initially. Your physician will advise you when an in-person visit is required for prescribing.

**Q: What if I need a telehealth visit but my DPC physician is unavailable?**
A: DPC practices handle coverage differently. Some have partners who cover for vacations and after-hours. Others use an answering service for true emergencies. Ask your specific practice about their coverage model before enrolling — this is an important question. For truly urgent situations when no coverage is available, urgent care or the ER remains appropriate.

**Q: Is DPC telehealth HIPAA compliant?**
A: Yes. DPC practices are required to use HIPAA-compliant communication platforms for telehealth visits. Text messaging may use secure messaging platforms rather than standard SMS. Ask your DPC practice about their specific communication tools — they should be able to explain what's secure and how patient privacy is protected.

Quick Answer

Telehealth in DPC is fundamentally different from generic telehealth platforms — it’s your own physician, who already knows your history, available to you by text, phone, or video as part of your membership with no per-visit fee. It’s the convenience of virtual care with the quality of a real relationship.

Explanation

Telehealth exploded during the pandemic, and many patients discovered that virtual visits could be genuinely useful. But most commercial telehealth platforms have a significant limitation: you’re connecting with a random physician who has never met you, doesn’t know your history, and will likely never see you again.

That physician follows a protocol. They’re cautious because they don’t know you. They prescribe conservatively, recommend you follow up with your “regular doctor,” and the interaction ends when the video call does.

Telehealth in a DPC practice is something different.

What Makes DPC Telehealth Different

When you have a video call, phone call, or text exchange with your DPC physician, the conversation begins with a foundation that commercial telehealth can never replicate: your doctor already knows you.

They know your medical history, your medication list, your allergies, your anxiety about certain symptoms, your family history, the chronic condition you’ve been managing, and the pattern of how you typically present when you’re sick. That context changes everything.

A physician who knows you can:

  • Make faster, more confident decisions — they’re not hedging because they lack context
  • Give you a real assessment instead of a defensive one — they know whether your symptoms fit a concerning pattern or a familiar benign one
  • Follow up — a DPC telehealth visit doesn’t end the relationship. Your physician will check in, review test results, and continue the conversation
  • Integrate the telehealth visit into your ongoing care — what happened during your telehealth visit is part of your record and informs future care

How DPC Telehealth Access Works

Different DPC practices structure telehealth access differently, but most offer some combination of:

Text messaging: For quick questions, photo review of a rash or wound, medication clarifications, or results questions, texting your physician directly is often the fastest path. This isn’t about firing off texts at midnight for non-urgent questions — it’s about having a professional communication channel that actually works.

Phone calls: For more nuanced conversations, a direct call with your physician is faster and more informative than the traditional “request a callback” approach. In most DPC practices, you’re reaching your physician directly, not a nurse or answering service.

Video visits: Full video appointments are available for clinical assessments that benefit from visual examination — skin conditions, eye issues, observing how a patient looks and moves, conversations requiring nuance. Video works especially well for mental health check-ins, medication management, chronic disease follow-up, and second opinions on whether an in-person visit is needed.

What Telehealth Handles Well in DPC

Many conditions and situations that generate in-person visits can be handled efficiently via telehealth:

  • Upper respiratory infections and flu assessment
  • UTI symptoms with prescription (where clinically appropriate)
  • Rashes and skin conditions (photo-based assessment)
  • Pink eye
  • Medication questions, side effects, and refills
  • Lab result review and interpretation
  • Mental health check-ins and medication management for anxiety and depression
  • Post-procedure or post-illness follow-up
  • Blood pressure or blood sugar review after home monitoring
  • Travel medicine consultations
  • Pre-visit preparation for upcoming specialist appointments

When In-Person Is Still Needed

Telehealth has real limits. Your DPC physician will ask you to come in when:

  • A physical examination is clinically necessary (abdominal pain, chest symptoms, joint injuries, anything requiring hands-on assessment)
  • Diagnostic testing needs to happen in the office (rapid strep or flu, urinalysis, EKG)
  • A concern warrants more careful observation than a video call allows
  • Immunizations, wound care, or minor procedures are needed

A good DPC physician will triage this honestly. They’ll tell you when they can handle something remotely and when they need to see you. Their goal is efficient, high-quality care — not driving unnecessary in-person visits.
